Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.front: What is the test for testamentary capacity and where does it come from?
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.answer:
The test is from Banks v Goodfellow (1870). The testator must: (1) understand the nature of making a will and its effects; (2) understand the extent of the property being disposed of; (3) understand the claims of those who might reasonably expect to benefit; and (4) not be suffering from any disorder of the mind that poisons the affections, perverts the sense of right, or prevents the exercise of natural faculties in disposing of property.

All four limbs must be satisfied at the time of execution. The test is of functional understanding — not a requirement of perfect memory or intelligence.
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.summary_line: TESTAMENTARY CAPACITY · BANKS V GOODFELLOW 1870 · FOUR LIMBS · UNDERSTAND PROPERTY · UNDERSTAND CLAIMS · NO DISORDER

Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.front: A testator suffers from dementia but has a lucid interval when she gives instructions for her will. The will is executed during a later lucid interval. Does she have testamentary capacity?
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.answer:
Yes, provided she satisfies the Banks v Goodfellow (1870) test at the time of execution. A person suffering from a chronic mental disorder may still have testamentary capacity during a lucid interval.

The 'Parker v Felgate' (1883) principle also applies: if the testator had capacity when she gave instructions, the will accurately reflects those instructions, and she understood at execution that she was signing a will prepared from her earlier instructions — even if she could no longer recall the detail — the will is valid.

Capacity is assessed at the date of execution, not at any earlier or later time.
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.summary_line: PARKER V FELGATE · LUCID INTERVAL · CAPACITY AT INSTRUCTIONS · VALID IF UNDERSTOOD SIGNING · DEMENTIA

Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.front: Who bears the burden of proving testamentary capacity, and how does it shift?
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.answer:
The burden of proving capacity lies on the person propounding the will (usually the executor or beneficiary seeking to uphold it): Tristram & Coote's Probate Practice.

However, where a will is rational on its face and duly executed, the court will presume capacity — the burden then shifts to the party challenging the will to raise evidence of incapacity.

Once genuine doubt is raised, the burden shifts back to the propounder to establish capacity on the balance of probabilities. Medical evidence and evidence of the solicitor who took instructions are typically decisive.
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.summary_line: BURDEN OF PROOF · PRESUMPTION FROM DUE EXECUTION · SHIFTS ON DOUBT · PROPOUNDER MUST PROVE · BALANCE OF PROBABILITIES

Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.front: What is the 'golden rule' in will-drafting practice for elderly or unwell testators, and what is its legal significance?
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.answer:
The 'golden rule' (Kenward v Adams [1975]) is that when a solicitor takes instructions for a will from an elderly or seriously ill testator, they should arrange for a medical practitioner to assess and record the testator's capacity at the time of execution.

Legal significance: the rule is not legally binding — failure to follow it does not automatically invalidate the will. However, a contemporaneous medical note is strong evidence of capacity if the will is later challenged. Conversely, failure to follow the rule when there were obvious warning signs may be relied on to cast doubt on the validity of the will and may expose the solicitor to professional criticism.

The rule is a practice standard, not a formal legal requirement.
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.summary_line: GOLDEN RULE · KENWARD V ADAMS · MEDICAL ASSESSMENT · BEST PRACTICE · NOT LEGALLY BINDING · ELDERLY TESTATOR

Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.issue: What is the test for testamentary capacity — what must a testator understand at the time of execution to make a valid will?
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.rule: The testator must: (1) understand the nature of making a will and its effects; (2) understand the extent of the property being disposed of; (3) understand the claims of those who might reasonably expect to benefit; and (4) not be suffering from any disorder of the mind that poisons the affections, perverts the sense of right, or prevents the exercise of the natural faculties in disposing of property. All four limbs must be satisfied at execution.
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.application: Banks suffered from a mental delusion (he believed he was being persecuted by a man long dead), but the court found this did not affect his ability to understand his property, his family, and the effects of his will. The delusion was not connected to the testamentary act.
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.conclusion: The will was valid. A general mental disorder does not destroy testamentary capacity unless it directly affects the testamentary act itself. This remains the foundational test for capacity in English law.
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.summary_line: BANKS V GOODFELLOW · FOUR LIMBS · MENTAL DISORDER · DELUSION · CAPACITY TEST · FUNCTIONAL UNDERSTANDING

Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.issue: Where a testator had capacity when giving instructions but lacked full capacity at the time of execution, is the will valid?
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.rule: A will is valid where: (1) the testator had capacity when they gave instructions to the solicitor; (2) the will was prepared in accordance with those instructions; and (3) at execution the testator understood they were signing a will prepared pursuant to instructions given when they had capacity — even if they could no longer recall the detail.
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.application: Parker gave detailed instructions when mentally competent but had deteriorated by the time of execution. The court found she understood at execution that she was giving effect to instructions she had earlier given, and that the will reflected her settled wishes.
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.conclusion: The will was valid. Parker v Felgate is a practical exception to the strict timing rule in Banks v Goodfellow — capacity at instruction-giving can suffice where understanding at execution is reduced but present in the limited sense described.
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.summary_line: PARKER V FELGATE · INSTRUCTIONS CAPACITY · REDUCED EXECUTION CAPACITY · UNDERSTANDING AT SIGNING · VALID WILL

Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.issue: What steps should a solicitor take when taking instructions from an elderly or seriously ill testator to protect the will against later challenge?
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.rule: The 'golden rule': when a solicitor takes instructions for a will from an elderly or seriously ill testator, they should arrange for a medical practitioner familiar with the testator to satisfy themselves of the testator's capacity and to record their findings. The rule is a practice standard — failure does not automatically invalidate the will but may be used as evidence of inadequate enquiry if capacity is later challenged.
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.application: The testator was elderly and ill. The solicitor prepared a will without arranging a medical assessment. After death, capacity was challenged. The court criticised the solicitor's failure to obtain contemporaneous medical evidence, making the determination of capacity more difficult.
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.conclusion: The case established the golden rule as a matter of best practice. A contemporaneous medical note provides the strongest protection against capacity challenges and is standard practice in modern solicitor-drafted wills for elderly or vulnerable clients.
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.summary_line: KENWARD V ADAMS · GOLDEN RULE · MEDICAL ASSESSMENT · BEST PRACTICE · NOT BINDING · ELDERLY TESTATOR

Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.front: Going deeper: The Banks v Goodfellow (1870) test for testamentary capacity was formulated in the Victorian era. Is it still fit for purpose in an ageing population where dementia, fluctuating capacity, and complex psychiatric conditions are increasingly common?
Testamentary capacity — Banks v Goodfell.answer:
The case for reform

Banks v Goodfellow was decided at a time when psychiatry was in its infancy and the population was significantly younger. The test asks whether the testator understood their property, their family, and the nature of making a will — a cognitive, not clinical, standard. Critics argue it is poorly adapted to:

1. Fluctuating conditions: dementia rarely produces a clear binary between capacity and incapacity. The test's snapshot approach (assessed at execution) fails to capture the reality of a progressive disorder.
2. Functional specificity: modern mental capacity law (Mental Capacity Act 2005) uses a decision-specific, functional approach — capacity is assessed for the particular decision at the particular time. Banks v Goodfellow predates this framework and sits uneasily alongside it.
3. Undue influence vulnerability: a testator may satisfy Banks v Goodfellow yet be highly susceptible to manipulation. The test is blind to this risk.

The Law Commission position

The Law Commission's project on Making a Will (launched 2017, consultation paper 2017) examined whether the Mental Capacity Act 2005 test should replace Banks v Goodfellow. The MCA 2005 test requires the decision-maker to understand, retain, and weigh relevant information and communicate a decision. Professor Penelope Reed KC (now retired from practice but a leading commentator) has argued this is functionally broader and more protective.

The counter-argument

Professor Ross Sherwood and others have argued that Banks v Goodfellow's very breadth — its qualitative rather than clinical framing — gives courts flexibility to assess capacity in a human rather than bureaucratic way. A rigid MCA 2005 framework might actually produce more invalid wills by setting a higher technical bar.

Current position

The Law Commission's reforms have not been enacted. Banks v Goodfellow remains the governing test. The golden rule (Kenward v Adams [1975]) and contemporary medical assessment are the practical responses to the gap — not legislative reform.

Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.front: What are the formal requirements for a valid will under s.9 Wills Act 1837 (as amended)?
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.summary_line: s.9 WILLS ACT 1837 · IN WRITING · SIGNED · INTENTION · TWO WITNESSES · PRESENCE · ATTEST AND SIGN

Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.front: The testator signs a will at the top of the first page rather than at the foot. Is the will valid?
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.answer:
Potentially yes. Section 9 Wills Act 1837 does not require the signature to appear at the foot — it requires that the testator intended by their signature to give effect to the will.

Under the pre-1983 position, a signature at the top would invalidate anything written below it. The Administration of Justice Act 1982 removed the requirement that the signature appear at the foot or end, replacing it with an intention test.

The will is valid if the court is satisfied the testator intended the signature to authenticate the whole document. Where a signature precedes the dispositive provisions, this may be harder to establish — but it is not automatically fatal.
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.summary_line: SIGNATURE POSITION · ADMINISTRATION OF JUSTICE ACT 1982 · INTENTION TEST · NOT FOOT ONLY · TOP SIGNATURE

Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.front: Can a will be signed by someone other than the testator? If so, what are the conditions?
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.answer:
Yes. Under s.9 Wills Act 1837, the will may be signed by some other person in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction.

Conditions: (1) the testator must be physically present; (2) the signature must be made at the testator's explicit direction; (3) the signing must occur in the testator's presence. The person signing on the testator's behalf can themselves be a witness, provided all other formality requirements are met.

This provision accommodates testators who cannot physically sign due to disability or illness. The testator's name need not be written in full — a mark or initials will suffice provided it is intended to constitute a signature.
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.summary_line: SIGNATURE BY ANOTHER · s.9 · TESTATOR'S PRESENCE · TESTATOR'S DIRECTION · DISABILITY · MARK SUFFICIENT

Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.front: Witness A signs the will in the same room as the testator but with their back turned while the testator signs. Witness B signs after seeing the testator sign. Is the will duly executed?
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.answer:
Witness A's attestation is likely invalid. The requirement that witnesses sign 'in the presence of' the testator is interpreted to include a line-of-sight test: the witness must be capable of seeing the act of signing if they chose to look — Casson v Dade (1781). A witness who has their back turned and could not have seen the testator sign does not satisfy this.

Witness B's attestation is valid on the facts — B witnessed the testator sign.

With only one valid witness, the will fails to comply with s.9 (which requires two witnesses). The will would be invalid. The solicitor preparing the will should have ensured both witnesses could observe the testator's signature.
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.summary_line: PRESENCE · LINE OF SIGHT · CASSON V DADE · BACK TURNED · ONE VALID WITNESS · WILL INVALID

Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.front: What is a donatio mortis causa (DMC) and what are the three conditions for a valid DMC?
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.answer:
A donatio mortis causa (DMC) is a gift made in contemplation of death — a conditional lifetime gift that becomes absolute on death without the formalities of a will or the administration process.

Three conditions (Sen v Headley [1991]; Cain v Moon [1896]):
1. The gift must be made in contemplation of death — the donor must be contemplating their own death in the near future from a specific cause (not merely the general possibility of death). Contemplation of suicide counts: Re Dudman [1925].
2. The gift must be conditional on death — it must be intended to take effect only if the donor dies. If the donor recovers, the gift is revoked automatically.
3. Delivery — the donor must part with dominion over the subject matter of the gift. Physical delivery of the asset (or the means of accessing it — e.g. handing over title deeds, a key, or a PIN) is required. A mere promise to give is insufficient.

A DMC does not require the formalities of s.9 Wills Act 1837. It takes effect outside the estate and outside the administration process — but it may still be subject to IHT as part of the deceased's estate.
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.summary_line: DONATIO MORTIS CAUSA · THREE CONDITIONS · CONTEMPLATION OF DEATH · CONDITIONAL · DELIVERY · SEN V HEADLEY

Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.front: What property can be the subject of a donatio mortis causa and what are the key limits?
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.answer:
Property capable of DMC: cash, chattels, cheques drawn on the donor's account, share certificates, land (confirmed in Sen v Headley [1991] — the House of Lords held that freehold land could be the subject of a DMC where title deeds were delivered).

Key limits:
1. The donor must have an interest capable of passing — a purely personal right (e.g. a contractual benefit that is non-assignable) cannot be the subject of a DMC.
2. Delivery must be of the subject matter or the means of control — handing over a key to a box containing the asset, or title deeds to land, satisfies the delivery requirement. A verbal promise alone does not.
3. The gift is automatically revoked if the donor recovers — the conditional nature is fundamental. A donor who survives can reclaim the property.
4. A DMC does not override a valid will — if the testator leaves the same property to someone else by will, the DMC takes priority (it passed at death outside the estate) only if all three conditions are satisfied.

IHT: the property subject to a DMC is treated as part of the deceased's estate for IHT purposes — it does not escape IHT simply by passing outside the administration process.
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.summary_line: DMC · CAPABLE PROPERTY · LAND · TITLE DEEDS · REVOKED ON RECOVERY · IHT APPLIES · NO WILL REQUIRED

Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.issue: What does it mean for a witness to sign a will 'in the presence of' the testator — is a line of sight required?
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.rule: A witness signs in the testator's presence if the testator could have seen the act of signing had they chosen to look — a line of sight test applies. The witness need not actually observe the testator's signature, but the testator must be capable of observing the witness's signature.
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.application: A witness signed in a carriage immediately outside the room where the testator sat. The testator could have seen through the window had they looked. The court found this satisfied the presence requirement.
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.conclusion: The will was validly attested. Casson v Dade establishes the line-of-sight test for presence. It cuts both ways — the reverse applies to the testator's signature being made in the witness's presence.
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.summary_line: CASSON V DADE · PRESENCE · LINE OF SIGHT · COULD HAVE SEEN · CARRIAGE · VALID ATTESTATION

Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.front: Going deeper: Secret trusts — where a testator leaves property to a legatee on the understanding that it will be held for a third party not named in the will — operate 'dehors the will' (outside the will). What is the theoretical justification for enforcing them and why does the debate matter?
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.answer:
The doctrine and its two forms

Fully secret trust: the will makes an outright gift to the legatee with no indication of a trust. The trust is communicated privately before death. The legatee holds the legal estate on trust for the undisclosed beneficiary.

Half-secret trust: the will acknowledges a trust exists but does not name the beneficiary. The terms are communicated privately.

The theoretical problem

Secret trusts appear to violate the Wills Act 1837 — they give effect to a testamentary intention without the formalities of s.9. Two justifications compete:

1. Fraud theory (dominant): the secret trustee would be perpetrating a fraud if they were permitted to take beneficially when they accepted the property on the basis of a trust. Equity will not allow the Wills Act to be used as an instrument of fraud: McCormick v Grogan (1869). The trust is enforced not as a testamentary disposition but to prevent unjust enrichment.

2. Dehors the will theory: secret trusts are entirely constituted inter vivos — the obligation arises from the communication and acceptance before death, not from the will itself. The will is merely the mechanism of transfer, not the source of the trust. Supported by Professor David Hodge KC and others; also consistent with Blackwell v Blackwell [1929].

Why the debate matters

The choice of theory produces different rules:
- On the fraud theory, the Wills Act is disapplied only to prevent fraud — suggesting the doctrine should be confined to cases of actual reliance.
- On the dehors theory, secret trusts are valid even without reliance — they are inter vivos trusts that happen to use a will as the vehicle of transfer.

Professor Penner (The Law of Trusts) argues neither theory is fully satisfactory: fraud theory overreaches (not all secret trustee windfalls are fraudulent in the moral sense); dehors theory is a fiction (the arrangement is plainly testamentary in substance).

The practical consequence: both fully secret and half-secret trusts are enforced in English law, but with different communication rules — half-secret trusts must be communicated before or at the time of the will (Blackwell v Blackwell), while fully secret trusts can be communicated at any time before death.

Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Walter is terminally ill. He calls his daughter Amy into his hospital room and says: 'Amy, I want you to have the house when I die.' He hands her the title deeds and says 'take these — it's yours when I'm gone.' Amy thanks him and leaves with the deeds. Walter dies two weeks later. A student advises that the gift of the house fails because it was not made by a valid will — Walter never signed a will in front of two witnesses.

Is the student correct?
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.issue: The Trap: the student correctly identifies that no valid will was made under s.9 Wills Act 1837. The student misses the possibility that the gift may be valid as a donatio mortis causa (DMC) — a recognised exception to the requirement for testamentary formality.
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.rule: The Reality: a donatio mortis causa (DMC) is a valid mechanism for passing property on death without a will, provided three conditions are satisfied: (1) the gift is made in contemplation of death from a specific cause; (2) the gift is conditional on death; and (3) the donor delivers dominion over the subject matter. Sen v Headley [1991] confirmed that freehold land can be the subject of a DMC where title deeds are delivered.
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.application: Why the Distractor Fails: all three DMC conditions appear satisfied — Walter is terminally ill (contemplation of death from a specific cause ✓); the gift is expressed to take effect 'when I'm gone' (conditional on death ✓); Walter physically handed over the title deeds (delivery of dominion ✓). The absence of a will is irrelevant — a DMC operates outside the will and outside the administration process. The student's error is treating wills formality as the only route to testamentary giving.
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.conclusion: The Key Rule: a DMC is a valid exception to the Wills Act formalities — if the three conditions (contemplation of death, conditionality, delivery) are all met, the gift is effective on death without a will. All three conditions must be present — if any one fails, the DMC fails.
Formalities — s.9 Wills Act 1837.summary_line: DONATIO MORTIS CAUSA · THREE CONDITIONS · NO WILL REQUIRED · SEN V HEADLEY · LAND INCLUDED

Knowledge and approval.front: What does it mean for a testator to have 'knowledge and approval' of a will's contents, and when is it presumed?
Knowledge and approval.answer:
Knowledge and approval means the testator understood and approved the specific contents of the will at the time of execution — not merely that they had testamentary capacity.

Presumption: where the testator had capacity and the will was duly executed, knowledge and approval is presumed. The propounder does not need to prove it affirmatively in every case.

The presumption is rebutted — and the court requires affirmative proof of knowledge and approval — where there are suspicious circumstances, particularly where the will was prepared by or at the instigation of a person who takes a substantial benefit under it: Wintle v Nye [1959]. In such cases the court scrutinises the evidence carefully.
Knowledge and approval.summary_line: KNOWLEDGE AND APPROVAL · PRESUMED FROM CAPACITY · REBUTTED BY SUSPICIOUS CIRCUMSTANCES · WINTLE V NYE · BURDEN

Knowledge and approval.front: A solicitor drafts a will under which they personally receive a substantial legacy. The testator signs. What issues arise and what should a solicitor do?
Knowledge and approval.answer:
Suspicious circumstances arise. Where a will was prepared by or at the instigation of a person who benefits substantially under it, the presumption of knowledge and approval is displaced: Wintle v Nye [1959]. The court will require affirmative proof that the testator understood and genuinely approved the gift.

SRA Code of Conduct implications: a solicitor must not accept a gift from a client in their will unless the client has received independent legal advice — Rule 6.1 SRA Code (Conflicts). The solicitor is in a position of potential conflict and must advise the client to seek independent advice before executing a will that benefits the solicitor.

Failure to do so risks the will being set aside and regulatory sanction.
Knowledge and approval.summary_line: SOLICITOR BENEFICIARY · SUSPICIOUS CIRCUMSTANCES · INDEPENDENT ADVICE · SRA CODE · WINTLE V NYE · GIFT AT RISK

Knowledge and approval.issue: Where the person who prepared the will is also a substantial beneficiary under it, what is the effect on the burden of proving knowledge and approval?
Knowledge and approval.rule: Where there are suspicious circumstances — particularly where the will was prepared by or at the instigation of a person who takes a substantial benefit — the presumption of knowledge and approval is displaced. The propounder must affirmatively prove that the testator knew and approved the contents of the will.
Knowledge and approval.application: Nye was a solicitor who prepared a will under which he received nearly the entire estate of an elderly client. The court found the circumstances were highly suspicious. Nye failed to discharge the burden of proving genuine knowledge and approval.
Knowledge and approval.conclusion: The will was set aside. Wintle v Nye establishes that suspicious circumstances shift the burden affirmatively to the propounder — a higher evidential threshold than the normal presumption of knowledge and approval from capacity and due execution.
Knowledge and approval.summary_line: WINTLE V NYE · SUSPICIOUS CIRCUMSTANCES · SOLICITOR BENEFICIARY · BURDEN SHIFTS · AFFIRMATIVE PROOF

Knowledge and approval.issue: Is knowledge and approval established merely by showing the will was read over to the testator before execution?
Knowledge and approval.rule: Knowledge and approval requires that the testator genuinely understood and approved the contents of the will — not merely that the formality of reading it was completed. Where a testator suffered from severe anxiety and the evidence showed they would have signed anything put before them without real comprehension, the presumption of knowledge and approval may be rebutted.
Knowledge and approval.application: Mrs Gill suffered from severe agoraphobia and anxiety. The will was read to her before execution but the Court of Appeal found she was in such a state that she lacked genuine understanding of what she was approving. The reading over was a formality without substance.
Knowledge and approval.conclusion: The will was set aside for want of knowledge and approval. Gill v Woodall is authority that the presumption can be rebutted by evidence of the testator's mental state at execution, even without formal incapacity under Banks v Goodfellow.
Knowledge and approval.summary_line: GILL V WOODALL · KNOWLEDGE AND APPROVAL · READ OVER INSUFFICIENT · ANXIETY · PRESUMPTION REBUTTED

Knowledge and approval.front: Going deeper: Will-writing is an unreserved legal activity — anyone may draft a will for a fee without qualification or regulation. Does this create an unacceptable consumer protection gap, and why has reform proved so difficult? (Cross-reference: FLK1-LS cards on reserved activities.)
Knowledge and approval.answer:
The consumer protection gap

Under Schedule 2 LSA 2007, will-drafting is not a reserved legal activity. Unregulated will writers are outside the SRA's jurisdiction, the Legal Ombudsman's compulsory scheme, and mandatory professional indemnity insurance requirements. The Institute of Professional Willwriters (IPW) operates a voluntary code — but membership is not compulsory.

The Law Commission (Intestacy and Family Provision Claims on Death, 2011) and the Legal Services Consumer Panel (2011) both identified will-writing as a significant consumer protection gap. Defective wills — including wills that fail for want of capacity, undue influence, or poor drafting — disproportionately harm beneficiaries who have no direct relationship with the will writer and discover the problem only after the testator's death.

Roger Kerridge (Durham, Parry and Clark: The Law of Succession) has argued that the stakes in will-drafting — the irreversible transfer of accumulated lifetime wealth — are at least as high as in conveyancing, which is reserved. The failure to reserve the activity reflects political economy rather than principled consumer protection analysis.

Why reform has stalled

Three structural obstacles:
1. Political economy: solicitors have a commercial interest in reservation (it would exclude cheaper competitors). Regulators have been reluctant to be seen to protect a professional monopoly — particularly given the post-LSA 2007 liberalisation agenda.
2. Proportionality: consumer harm from defective wills is diffuse and delayed — it materialises after death, affects beneficiaries rather than clients, and is hard to aggregate into compelling statistics.
3. Incremental alternatives: approved scheme operators and voluntary codes have been proposed as lighter-touch responses — less disruptive than full reservation.

The comparison with conveyancing is instructive but imperfect: in conveyancing, the client is the person who suffers if the transaction fails. In will-drafting, the client (the testator) is dead before the harm emerges — a different consumer protection calculus that has made political intervention harder to justify.

⚠️ Link — Reserved legal activities / will-writing (see FLK1-LS cards)

Knowledge and approval.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Bridget, aged 88, makes a will in favour of her carer. The will was prepared by a solicitor who is unconnected to the carer. Bridget had full testamentary capacity under Banks v Goodfellow at the time of execution. The will was read to her and she signed it in front of two independent witnesses. After Bridget's death, her family challenges the will. A student advises that the challenge must fail — Bridget had capacity and the will was properly executed, so knowledge and approval is conclusively established.

Is the student correct?
Knowledge and approval.issue: The Trap: the student correctly identifies capacity and due execution, and correctly knows that these ordinarily give rise to a presumption of knowledge and approval. The student treats the presumption as conclusive — missing that it can be rebutted by suspicious circumstances.
Knowledge and approval.rule: The Reality: knowledge and approval is presumed from capacity and due execution — but the presumption is rebuttable. Where suspicious circumstances exist, the presumption is displaced and the propounder must affirmatively prove knowledge and approval: Wintle v Nye [1959], Gill v Woodall [2010]. A gift to a carer is a recognised suspicious circumstance — even where the solicitor is independent.
Knowledge and approval.application: Why the Distractor Fails: the burden analysis has two stages: (1) due execution + capacity → presumption of knowledge and approval arises; (2) suspicious circumstances (gift to carer, elderly and potentially vulnerable testator) → presumption is displaced and the burden shifts to the propounder to prove knowledge and approval affirmatively. Capacity and due execution are necessary but not sufficient where suspicious circumstances exist. The family's challenge is not automatically defeated — the executor must adduce positive evidence that Bridget genuinely understood and approved the gift to the carer.
Knowledge and approval.conclusion: The Key Rule: the presumption of knowledge and approval from capacity and due execution is rebuttable — suspicious circumstances (especially a gift to a person in a position of influence) displace it and require affirmative proof.
Knowledge and approval.summary_line: KNOWLEDGE AND APPROVAL · PRESUMPTION REBUTTABLE · SUSPICIOUS CIRCUMSTANCES · CARER GIFT · BURDEN SHIFTS

Undue influence and fraud.front: How does undue influence in the context of wills differ from undue influence in contract law?
Undue influence and fraud.answer:
In contract law, undue influence may be presumed from a relationship of trust and confidence — the burden shifts to the stronger party to rebut it (Royal Bank of Scotland v Etridge [2001]).

In the law of wills, there is no presumption of undue influence from a relationship alone. Undue influence must be proved affirmatively: Hall v Hall (1868). The influence must amount to coercion — it must overpower the testator's volition so that the will is not truly theirs.

Mere persuasion, appeals to affection, or pressure that stops short of coercion do not constitute undue influence in the testamentary context. This is a significantly higher bar than the contractual doctrine.
Undue influence and fraud.summary_line: UNDUE INFLUENCE · COERCION NOT PERSUASION · NO PRESUMPTION · HALL V HALL · TESTAMENTARY VS CONTRACT

Undue influence and fraud.front: What is fraud in the context of wills, and what are its two main forms?
Undue influence and fraud.answer:
Fraud in the context of wills operates to invalidate a will or specific gifts where the testator's decision was procured by misrepresentation.

Two main forms:
1. Fraud as to the nature of the document — the testator is deceived into signing something they believe to be a different document (e.g. a power of attorney). The entire will is void.
2. Fraud as to the character or conduct of a beneficiary or potential beneficiary — false representations made to the testator to induce or prevent a gift. Only the gift procured by the fraud fails; the rest of the will stands.

Forged wills are void ab initio. The burden of proving fraud lies on the party alleging it.
Undue influence and fraud.summary_line: FRAUD · NATURE OF DOCUMENT · CHARACTER FRAUD · GIFT ONLY FAILS · VOID AB INITIO · BURDEN ON CHALLENGER

Undue influence and fraud.issue: What level of influence over a testator constitutes undue influence sufficient to invalidate a will?
Undue influence and fraud.rule: Undue influence in the testamentary context requires actual coercion — the influence must overpower the volition of the testator without convincing their judgment. Mere persuasion, appeals to natural affection, or pressure which falls short of coercion does not constitute undue influence in a will context. There is no presumption of undue influence from a relationship of trust and confidence.
Undue influence and fraud.application: The testator made a will in favour of one of his children. Other family members challenged it on the basis of influence. The court found persuasion and moral pressure had been applied but this fell well short of coercion — the testator's will remained his own.
Undue influence and fraud.conclusion: No undue influence. Hall v Hall sets the high threshold for testamentary undue influence — a critical contrast with the lower contractual standard where presumption operates from a relationship of trust.
Undue influence and fraud.summary_line: HALL V HALL · UNDUE INFLUENCE · COERCION · NOT PERSUASION · NO PRESUMPTION · HIGH THRESHOLD · TESTAMENTARY

Undue influence and fraud.issue: What evidence is required to prove undue influence in a testamentary context and how does the burden operate?
Undue influence and fraud.rule: Undue influence must be proved by the party alleging it — there is no presumption. The standard is the balance of probabilities. The influence must amount to coercion that overpowers the testator's free will. Suspicious circumstances alone are insufficient; there must be evidence that the will does not represent the testator's own wishes.
Undue influence and fraud.application: A father made a will excluding his son. The son alleged undue influence by his sister. The evidence showed a difficult family relationship and some pressure but no coercion. The Court of Appeal upheld the will — there was insufficient evidence that the father's decision was not his own.
Undue influence and fraud.conclusion: The will was upheld. Fuller v Strum confirms the high evidential bar for testamentary undue influence and the absence of any presumption from a family relationship, contrasting clearly with the contractual doctrine.
Undue influence and fraud.summary_line: FULLER V STRUM · UNDUE INFLUENCE · BALANCE OF PROBABILITIES · FAMILY PRESSURE · WILL UPHELD

Undue influence and fraud.front: Going deeper: The threshold for testamentary undue influence — requiring actual coercion rather than mere presumption — is significantly higher than the contractual standard. Is this asymmetry justified, and does it leave vulnerable testators insufficiently protected?
Undue influence and fraud.answer:
The asymmetry

In contract law, undue influence may be presumed from a relationship of trust and confidence, shifting the burden to the stronger party: Royal Bank of Scotland v Etridge [2001]. In wills, no such presumption arises — the challenger must prove coercion on the balance of probabilities: Hall v Hall (1868), Fuller v Strum [2001].

The traditional justification

The higher testamentary bar reflects: (1) testamentary freedom — English law is reluctant to override a testator's settled wishes; (2) the evidential problem — the testator is dead and cannot give evidence; (3) the distinction between persuasion and coercion — family members naturally seek to influence wills and the law should not discourage this.

Professor Birks (Unjust Enrichment) argued the asymmetry was principled: testamentary dispositions are gratuitous — there is no counterparty who is enriched at the testator's expense in the way a contracting party is. The wrong (if any) is done to the testator's autonomy, not to a counterparty's rights.

The case against the asymmetry

Professor Mindy Chen-Wishart (Contract Law) and others have questioned whether the distinction serves vulnerable testators. An elderly person subject to sustained low-level manipulation by a carer or family member may be profoundly influenced without meeting the coercion threshold. The knowledge and approval doctrine (Gill v Woodall [2010]) partially fills this gap — but it addresses comprehension rather than manipulation.

The unresolved tension

The Law Commission's 2017 consultation paper on Making a Will invited views on whether a presumption of undue influence should be introduced in cases where a beneficiary played a significant role in preparing or executing the will. No reform has followed. The golden rule (Kenward v Adams) remains the primary prophylactic — a structural solution (medical evidence at execution) rather than a doctrinal one.

Revocation, alteration and revival.front: By what methods may a will be revoked?
Revocation, alteration and revival.summary_line: REVOCATION · MARRIAGE · LATER WILL · WRITTEN DECLARATION · PHYSICAL DESTRUCTION · s.18 · s.20 · DIVORCE LAPSE

Revocation, alteration and revival.front: The testator tears up their will intending to revoke it, but later changes their mind and tapes the pieces back together. Is the will revoked?
Revocation, alteration and revival.answer:
Yes, the will is revoked. Revocation by physical destruction under s.20 Wills Act 1837 requires: (1) the physical act of destruction; and (2) the intention to revoke at the time of destruction (animus revocandi).

Both elements were satisfied at the moment of tearing. Revocation is complete at that point. The subsequent change of mind and physical reassembly cannot undo a completed revocation — there is no 'un-revocation' by physical act alone.

To restore the testamentary position, the testator must execute a new will. If the contents of the destroyed will can be proved (e.g. from a copy), a court may order a grant of probate of the reconstructed will — but the original revocation stands.
Revocation, alteration and revival.summary_line: PHYSICAL DESTRUCTION · ANIMUS REVOCANDI · ACCIDENTAL NO EFFECT · REASSEMBLY INSUFFICIENT · NEW WILL NEEDED

Revocation, alteration and revival.front: How can a revoked will be revived, and what are the limits?
Revocation, alteration and revival.answer:
A revoked will can only be revived by re-execution with the full formalities of s.9 Wills Act 1837, or by a duly executed codicil showing an intention to revive it: s.22 Wills Act 1837.

Key limits:
- A will revoked by a later will cannot be revived simply by destroying the later will — the old will remains revoked: s.22.
- Re-execution or a revival codicil must clearly show the intention to revive the earlier will.
- Where a will was revoked by destruction, revival requires a new will (re-execution of the destroyed document is impossible).

A codicil that incorporates an earlier will by reference treats the earlier will as republished — it speaks from the date of the codicil.
Revocation, alteration and revival.summary_line: REVIVAL · s.22 · RE-EXECUTION · CODICIL · DESTRUCTION OF LATER WILL INSUFFICIENT · REPUBLICATION

Revocation, alteration and revival.front: Going deeper: Mutual wills — where two parties (usually spouses) agree to make wills in agreed terms and not to revoke them — are one of the most contested doctrines in succession law. What is the theoretical basis for the doctrine and why does it remain controversial?
Revocation, alteration and revival.answer:
The doctrine in outline

Mutual wills arise where two persons agree to make wills in particular terms and further agree not to revoke them without the other's consent. When the first party dies and the survivor benefits, equity imposes a constructive trust over the survivor's estate to give effect to the agreed terms: Re Hagger [1930], Re Green [1951], Birch v Curtis [2002].

The theoretical dispute

Three competing bases have been advanced:

1. Contract theory: the agreement not to revoke is a contract. On the first party's death, the contract becomes irrevocable. This is supported by Dufour v Pereira (1769) but sits awkwardly with the rule that a will is always revocable — statute (s.18A Wills Act 1837) cannot be contracted out of.

2. Constructive trust theory: the trust arises not from contract but from the unconscionability of the survivor retaining the benefit without honouring the agreement. This is the dominant modern analysis: Gillett v Holt [2001] (though a proprietary estoppel case, the unconscionability analysis is transferable). The constructive trust is imposed on the survivor's estate from the moment of the first death.

3. Equitable fraud theory: the survivor who takes the benefit of the first will and then revokes their own will is perpetrating a fraud on the deceased's estate — equity intervenes to prevent unjust enrichment.

The unresolved questions

Professor David Hayton (Underhill & Hayton) has identified three unsettled issues: (1) precisely when does the constructive trust arise — on the agreement, on the first death, or on the survivor receiving the benefit?; (2) does the trust bind the survivor's entire estate or only the assets received from the first death?; (3) what constitutes sufficient agreement — must it be express or can it be inferred?

Re Goodchild [1997] confirmed that a mere mirror will (two wills in the same terms) does not without more give rise to mutual wills — there must be a binding agreement. This limits the doctrine but leaves its doctrinal basis unresolved.

Revocation, alteration and revival.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Ian's will is stored in his home. A house fire destroys the property and all its contents, including the will. Ian survives but dies six months later without making a new will. His executor locates a photocopy of the will. A student advises that the will was revoked by the fire and Ian died intestate — the photocopy is irrelevant.

Is the student right?
Revocation, alteration and revival.issue: The Trap: the student assumes physical destruction of a will always constitutes revocation. The scenario involves accidental destruction — not deliberate destruction by the testator.
Revocation, alteration and revival.rule: The Reality: revocation by physical destruction under s.20 Wills Act 1837 requires two elements: (1) the physical act of destruction; AND (2) the intention to revoke (animus revocandi) at the time of destruction. Accidental destruction satisfies the physical act but not the mental element — there is no animus revocandi.
Revocation, alteration and revival.application: Why the Distractor Fails: the fire destroyed the will accidentally — Ian did not intend to revoke it. Without animus revocandi, there is no revocation. The will remains valid in law. The photocopy is highly relevant — the court can order a grant of probate of a reconstructed will where the contents can be proved, even if the original is lost or accidentally destroyed: Sugden v Lord St Leonards (1876).
Revocation, alteration and revival.conclusion: The Key Rule: accidental destruction does not revoke a will — both the physical act and the intention to revoke must coexist at the moment of destruction. A lost or accidentally destroyed will can be proved by secondary evidence.
Revocation, alteration and revival.summary_line: ACCIDENTAL DESTRUCTION · NO ANIMUS REVOCANDI · WILL SURVIVES · SECONDARY EVIDENCE · PROBATE OF COPY

Revocation, alteration and revival.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

In 2020, James makes a will containing the clause 'I make this will in contemplation of my forthcoming marriage to Karen'. In 2022, James and Karen end their relationship. In 2023, James marries Lisa. James dies in 2025 without making a new will. A student advises that the 2020 will survives because it was made 'in contemplation of marriage' and is therefore protected from automatic revocation.

Is the student correct?
Revocation, alteration and revival.issue: The Trap: the student correctly identifies that s.18(3) Wills Act 1837 protects a will made in contemplation of a specific marriage from automatic revocation by that marriage. The student then assumes this protection extends to any subsequent marriage.
Revocation, alteration and revival.rule: The Reality: section 18(3) protects a will from revocation only by the specific marriage it contemplates. The 2020 will contemplated marriage to Karen — not to Lisa. James's marriage to Lisa in 2023 is a different marriage and automatically revokes the 2020 will under s.18 Wills Act 1837.
Revocation, alteration and revival.application: Why the Distractor Fails: the 'in contemplation of marriage' exception is marriage-specific. The clause naming Karen does not protect the will from revocation by a later marriage to a different person. James dies having been revoked by his marriage to Lisa, with no valid will. His estate passes on intestacy — Lisa, as surviving spouse, is entitled under the AEA 1925.
Revocation, alteration and revival.conclusion: The Key Rule: s.18(3) protects against revocation by the specific contemplated marriage only — a later marriage to a different person revokes the will in the usual way.
Revocation, alteration and revival.summary_line: s.18(3) · CONTEMPLATION OF SPECIFIC MARRIAGE · DIFFERENT MARRIAGE REVOKES · INTESTACY

Capacity / Parker v Felgate scenarios.front: Arthur, aged 82, has been diagnosed with moderate vascular dementia. His solicitor visits him at home and takes instructions for a new will leaving everything to his daughter. During the meeting Arthur is lucid, recalls his assets accurately, and understands that his son will receive nothing. Three weeks later the solicitor attends for execution. Arthur appears confused and cannot remember the details of the will, but when the solicitor reads it to him Arthur says 'yes, that's what I want — I said so last time'. Both witnesses sign in Arthur's presence. Is the will valid?
Capacity / Parker v Felgate scenarios.answer:
The will is likely valid. Two routes support validity:

1. Banks v Goodfellow (1870): if Arthur had capacity at execution — understanding the nature of the will, his property, and the claims of others — the will is valid despite his dementia. The facts suggest he did not satisfy all four limbs at execution (he could not recall details), so this route is uncertain.

2. Parker v Felgate (1883): Arthur had full capacity when he gave instructions. The will was prepared in accordance with those instructions. At execution, Arthur understood he was signing a will made pursuant to his earlier instructions — demonstrated by his statement. This satisfies Parker v Felgate even if he lacked Banks v Goodfellow capacity at execution.

Formalities under s.9 Wills Act 1837 are satisfied on the facts. The solicitor should have followed the Kenward v Adams golden rule and obtained a medical assessment — without it, the validity of the will may be harder to defend if challenged.
Capacity / Parker v Felgate scenarios.summary_line: PARKER V FELGATE · CAPACITY AT INSTRUCTIONS · DEMENTIA · GOLDEN RULE · VALID WILL · TWO ROUTES

Capacity / Parker v Felgate scenarios.front: Beatrice has a longstanding paranoid delusion that her son Marcus is trying to steal her money. In all other respects she is mentally competent. She makes a will leaving her entire estate to charity and excluding Marcus. At execution she correctly identifies all her assets, understands she is making a will, and understands her family members' claims. Does her delusion affect the will's validity?
Capacity / Parker v Felgate scenarios.answer:
The will is likely valid. Under Banks v Goodfellow (1870), the fourth limb requires that the testator not suffer from a disorder of the mind that 'poisons the affections, perverts the sense of right, or prevents the exercise of the natural faculties in disposing of property'.

The question is whether the delusion directly caused or influenced the testamentary decision — i.e. whether but for the delusion, Beatrice would have included Marcus. If the gift to charity and exclusion of Marcus was independently motivated (e.g. she has always supported charity; her relationship with Marcus has been poor for reasons unconnected to the delusion), the will is valid.

If, however, the delusion about Marcus specifically caused his exclusion, the fourth Banks v Goodfellow limb is not met as to that decision — the gift to charity would stand but the exclusion of Marcus might be challenged.

This is a fact-sensitive analysis. Banks v Goodfellow itself was decided in favour of validity on similar facts (the testator had an irrational belief but it did not affect the dispositions).
Capacity / Parker v Felgate scenarios.summary_line: BANKS V GOODFELLOW · DELUSION · FOURTH LIMB · CONNECTED TO DISPOSITION · FACT SENSITIVE

Capacity / Parker v Felgate scenarios.front: Clara instructs her solicitor to prepare a will. The solicitor, who is named as a beneficiary receiving £20,000, drafts the will without advising Clara to seek independent legal advice. Clara signs. Clara subsequently dies. Her family challenges the will. What issues arise and what is the likely outcome?
Capacity / Parker v Felgate scenarios.answer:
Two distinct issues arise:

1. Knowledge and approval — Wintle v Nye [1959]: the solicitor prepared a will under which they personally benefit. This creates suspicious circumstances, displacing the presumption of knowledge and approval. The propounder (executor) must affirmatively prove Clara genuinely understood and approved the gift to the solicitor. Given the solicitor's conflict, this will be difficult to establish without independent evidence of Clara's settled intention.

2. SRA Code of Conduct: Rule 6.1 prohibits a solicitor from accepting a gift from a client in a will unless the client has received independent legal advice. The solicitor breached this rule by failing to advise Clara to seek independent advice. This is a professional conduct matter regardless of the will's validity.

Likely outcome: the gift of £20,000 to the solicitor may be set aside for want of knowledge and approval (or on public policy grounds), even if the rest of the will is upheld. The solicitor faces regulatory sanction. The rest of the will may stand if valid.
Capacity / Parker v Felgate scenarios.summary_line: SOLICITOR BENEFICIARY · WINTLE V NYE · SUSPICIOUS CIRCUMSTANCES · SRA CODE · INDEPENDENT ADVICE · GIFT AT RISK

Formalities / s.9 edge cases.front: David signs his will in the presence of two witnesses — his neighbour and his neighbour's 16-year-old daughter. Both watch him sign and then sign the will themselves in his presence. David is 30 and has full capacity. Is the will validly executed?
Formalities / s.9 edge cases.answer:
Yes, the will is validly executed. Section 9 Wills Act 1837 requires two witnesses but imposes no minimum age requirement for witnesses. A 16-year-old can validly witness a will.

All s.9 requirements are met: (1) the will is in writing; (2) David signed it; (3) he intended the signature to give effect to the will; (4) both witnesses were present at the same time when he signed; (5) both witnesses signed in his presence.

Note: the 16-year-old's status as a witness is irrelevant to the will's validity. However, if the 16-year-old were also a beneficiary under the will, s.15 Wills Act 1837 would void that gift — the witness's age does not protect the gift.
Formalities / s.9 edge cases.summary_line: s.9 · MINOR WITNESS · NO AGE REQUIREMENT · VALID EXECUTION · s.15 IF BENEFICIARY

Formalities / s.9 edge cases.front: Eve is physically unable to sign her will due to severe arthritis. She asks her carer, Frank, to sign on her behalf. Frank signs 'Frank (on behalf of Eve)' in the presence of Eve and two witnesses. The witnesses then sign in Eve's presence. Is the will valid?
Formalities / s.9 edge cases.answer:
Yes, the will is validly executed. Section 9 Wills Act 1837 expressly permits a will to be signed by some other person in the testator's presence and by the testator's direction.

Conditions satisfied: (1) Frank signed in Eve's presence; (2) he did so at her direction; (3) two witnesses then attested in Eve's presence.

Note: Frank, as the person who signed on Eve's behalf, can also act as one of the two witnesses — s.9 does not disqualify him. However, if Frank is also a beneficiary, his attestation as a witness would void his gift under s.15 Wills Act 1837.

The form of signature ('Frank on behalf of Eve') is acceptable — no particular form is required provided it is intended to constitute the testator's signature.
Formalities / s.9 edge cases.summary_line: SIGNATURE BY ANOTHER · s.9 · CARER · TESTATOR'S PRESENCE · TESTATOR'S DIRECTION · FRANK CAN WITNESS

Formalities / s.9 edge cases.front: George makes a will in 2018. In 2022 he marries Harriet. He does not make a new will. George dies in 2025. What is the effect on the 2018 will?
Formalities / s.9 edge cases.answer:
The 2018 will is automatically revoked by the marriage to Harriet: s.18 Wills Act 1837. Marriage revokes a will unless the will was made in contemplation of that specific marriage — s.18(3) Wills Act 1837.

There is nothing on the facts to suggest the 2018 will was made in contemplation of the marriage to Harriet (which took place four years later). The will is therefore wholly revoked.

George dies intestate. His estate passes under the Administration of Estates Act 1925 intestacy rules. Harriet, as surviving spouse, is entitled to personal chattels, the statutory legacy (£322,000), and half the residue. Any remaining half passes on the statutory trusts — to issue if any, or otherwise to Harriet absolutely (since there is no issue mentioned, Harriet would take the whole estate).
Formalities / s.9 edge cases.summary_line: MARRIAGE REVOKES WILL · s.18 · CONTEMPLATION SPECIFIC MARRIAGE · INTESTACY · SPOUSE TAKES

Revocation.front: Ivan makes a will in 2019 leaving everything to his brother. In 2022 he makes a new will leaving everything to his sister, which includes a clause 'I revoke all previous wills'. In 2024 Ivan destroys the 2022 will in anger, intending to revoke it. He dies in 2025 having made no further will. What is his testamentary position?
Revocation.answer:
Ivan dies intestate. The analysis proceeds in stages:

1. The 2022 will expressly revoked the 2019 will — the 2019 will is gone.
2. Ivan destroyed the 2022 will with animus revocandi (intention to revoke) — the 2022 will is revoked by physical destruction under s.20 Wills Act 1837.
3. Destruction of the 2022 will does not revive the 2019 will — s.22 Wills Act 1837: a will once revoked is not revived by the mere destruction of the later revoking will. Revival requires re-execution or a codicil showing an intention to revive.

Ivan dies with no valid will. His estate passes under the intestacy rules. His brother and sister may both have entitlements depending on whether other relatives (spouse, children, parents) survive.
Revocation.summary_line: REVOCATION · DESTRUCTION · s.22 · LATER WILL REVOKES EARLIER · DESTRUCTION DOES NOT REVIVE · INTESTACY

Revocation.front: Julia's will is lost after her death. Her executor believes it was accidentally destroyed by Julia's cleaner. Julia's family argues the will was deliberately destroyed by Julia to revoke it. Who bears the burden of proof and what is the presumption?
Revocation.answer:
Where a will was last known to be in the testator's possession but cannot be found after death, there is a rebuttable presumption that the testator destroyed it with the intention to revoke it: Welch v Phillips (1836).

Burden: the burden of rebutting the presumption falls on the person seeking to propound the will (here, the executor). They must prove on the balance of probabilities that the destruction was accidental — not deliberate.

Evidence relevant to rebutting the presumption includes: a copy of the will, evidence of the testator's settled intention to maintain the will, evidence that the testator was unable to access the will (e.g. it was held by the solicitor), and evidence of the circumstances of loss.

If the copy or contents of the will can be proved, the court may order a grant of probate of the reconstructed will — but only if the presumption of destruction is successfully rebutted.
Revocation.summary_line: LOST WILL · PRESUMPTION DESTROYED · REBUTTABLE · BURDEN ON PROPOUNDER · COPY EVIDENCE · PROBATE

Witnessing and s.15.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Ellen makes a will leaving £40,000 to her friend Fred and the residue to her daughter Grace. Fred's husband, Henry, witnesses the will alongside a neighbour. Ellen's executor applies for probate. A student advises that the will is invalid because Henry — a witness — is married to a beneficiary, meaning the will was not properly witnessed, and the estate must pass on intestacy.

Is the student correct?
Witnessing and s.15.issue: The Trap: the student concludes the will is invalid because a beneficiary's spouse witnessed it. The student conflates the effect on the gift with the effect on the will's validity.
Witnessing and s.15.rule: The Reality: section 15 Wills Act 1837 voids the gift to the witness's spouse — it does not invalidate the will or invalidate the attestation. Henry's signature still counts as valid attestation for the purposes of s.9 Wills Act 1837. The will is admitted to probate.
Witnessing and s.15.application: Why the Distractor Fails: the relevant question under s.15 is whether the gift to the beneficiary fails — not whether the will fails. The will has two witnesses (Henry and the neighbour) — both attestations are valid. Only Fred's gift (as spouse of a witness) is void. Grace takes the residue (which now includes the £40,000). The estate does not pass on intestacy.
Witnessing and s.15.conclusion: The Key Rule: s.15 voids the gift to the witness or their spouse — the will stands, the attestation counts, and only the specific gift fails.
Witnessing and s.15.summary_line: s.15 · GIFT VOID NOT WILL · ATTESTATION VALID · RESIDUE ABSORBS FAILED GIFT

Spouse/civil partner entitlement.front: What does a surviving spouse or civil partner receive on intestacy where the deceased leaves issue?
Spouse/civil partner entitlement.answer:
Under the Administration of Estates Act 1925 (as amended by the Inheritance and Trustees' Powers Act 2014):

- The surviving spouse/civil partner takes all personal chattels absolutely;
- A statutory legacy (currently £322,000, subject to periodic revision);
- One half of the remainder of the residuary estate absolutely.

The other half of the residuary estate passes on the statutory trusts for issue (per stirpes).

If the estate is worth less than the statutory legacy, the spouse takes the whole estate absolutely. The issue take nothing.

⚠️ Link — Statutory trusts for issue (see FLK2-WTP-019)
Spouse/civil partner entitlement.summary_line: INTESTACY · SPOUSE + ISSUE · PERSONAL CHATTELS · STATUTORY LEGACY £322K · HALF RESIDUE · AEA 1925

Spouse/civil partner entitlement.front: What does a surviving spouse or civil partner receive on intestacy where the deceased leaves no issue?
Spouse/civil partner entitlement.answer:
Where the intestate leaves a spouse or civil partner but no issue, the surviving spouse/civil partner takes the whole estate absolutely: Administration of Estates Act 1925, s.46 (as amended by ITPA 2014).

This applies regardless of whether other relatives (parents, siblings) survive. They take nothing if there is a surviving spouse and no issue.

Note the contrast with the pre-2014 position (which gave parents a share in certain circumstances). Since October 2014, the spouse takes everything if there is no issue.
Spouse/civil partner entitlement.summary_line: INTESTACY · SPOUSE NO ISSUE · WHOLE ESTATE · s.46 AEA 1925 · PARENTS AND SIBLINGS EXCLUDED

Spouse/civil partner entitlement.front: The intestate dies survived by a spouse and two children from a previous relationship. The estate is worth £500,000. How is the estate distributed?
Spouse/civil partner entitlement.answer:
Apply the intestacy rules under s.46 AEA 1925:

1. Personal chattels → spouse absolutely.
2. Statutory legacy (£322,000) → spouse absolutely.
3. Residue = £500,000 − £322,000 = £178,000 (ignoring chattels value for simplicity).
4. Half of residue (£89,000) → spouse absolutely.
5. Half of residue (£89,000) → held on statutory trusts for the two children equally (£44,500 each).

The children's share is held on trust until age 18 (or earlier marriage/civil partnership). If a child predeceases leaving issue of their own, those issue take the deceased child's share per stirpes.
Spouse/civil partner entitlement.summary_line: INTESTACY CALCULATION · £500K · STATUTORY LEGACY · HALF RESIDUE · CHILDREN PER STIRPES

Spouse/civil partner entitlement.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Oliver dies intestate with an estate of £600,000. He is survived by his wife Paula and their three children. A student advises that Paula, as surviving spouse, takes the entire estate of £600,000 absolutely because a surviving spouse always inherits everything on intestacy.

Is the student correct?
Spouse/civil partner entitlement.issue: The Trap: the student overgeneralises the intestacy rules for surviving spouses. The rule that a spouse takes everything only applies where there are no surviving issue. The student ignores the children.
Spouse/civil partner entitlement.rule: The Reality: under s.46 AEA 1925, where the intestate leaves both a surviving spouse and issue, the spouse does not take the entire estate. The spouse takes: personal chattels, the statutory legacy (£322,000), and half the residue. The other half of the residue passes on the statutory trusts for issue.
Spouse/civil partner entitlement.application: Why the Distractor Fails: the 'spouse takes everything' rule applies only where there is a surviving spouse but no issue. On these facts, Oliver has three children — issue survive him. Paula takes chattels + £322,000 + half of £278,000 (= £139,000) = approximately £461,000. The three children share the remaining £139,000 (approximately £46,333 each). Paula does not take £600,000.
Spouse/civil partner entitlement.conclusion: The Key Rule: a surviving spouse takes the whole estate on intestacy only where there are no surviving issue. Where issue survive, the statutory legacy formula applies and the issue share the residue.
Spouse/civil partner entitlement.summary_line: SPOUSE + ISSUE · STATUTORY LEGACY · HALF RESIDUE TO SPOUSE · HALF TO ISSUE · NOT WHOLE ESTATE

Statutory trusts for issue.front: What are the statutory trusts for issue and how does the per stirpes distribution work?
Statutory trusts for issue.answer:
Where issue take on intestacy, their share is held on the statutory trusts under s.47 AEA 1925. Issue share the estate (or their portion of it) equally at the first degree, but the share of any deceased child passes to their own issue per stirpes.

Per stirpes: each branch of the family takes the share the deceased parent would have received, divided equally among the members of that branch.

Example: the intestate has two children, A and B. B predeceased leaving two children (X and Y). The estate passes: half to A; and half to the B-branch — so X and Y take one quarter each.

Beneficiaries must attain 18 or marry/form a civil partnership before 18 to become entitled absolutely. Until then, PRs hold on trust.
Statutory trusts for issue.summary_line: STATUTORY TRUSTS · s.47 AEA 1925 · PER STIRPES · EQUAL AT FIRST DEGREE · AGE 18 · DECEASED CHILD REPRESENTED

Statutory trusts for issue.front: An intestate dies leaving no spouse but three children: A (alive), B (predeceased, leaving one child C), and D (predeceased, leaving no issue). Estate = £180,000. How is this distributed?
Statutory trusts for issue.answer:
Three children were entitled at first degree. D predeceased and left no issue — D's share fails and falls back into the estate to be redistributed.

Effectively only two branches survive:
- A's branch
- B's branch (represented by C)

Distribution:
- A: £90,000 absolutely.
- C: £90,000 (taking B's share per stirpes), held on trust until 18.
- D's potential share: since D left no issue, D cannot be represented. The estate is divided between the two surviving branches only.

Note: the estate is divided equally at the first degree among those children with living issue or who are alive — branches with no surviving issue simply drop out.
Statutory trusts for issue.summary_line: PER STIRPES · DECEASED CHILD NO ISSUE · DROPS OUT · SURVIVING BRANCHES SHARE · INTESTACY CALCULATION

Statutory trusts for issue.front: Does a child born outside marriage have the same intestacy rights as a child born within marriage?
Statutory trusts for issue.answer:
Yes. Section 1 Family Law Reform Act 1969 (as amended) provides that references to a person's children and other relatives in the Administration of Estates Act 1925 include illegitimate relationships. This was reinforced by the Family Law Reform Act 1987.

A child born outside marriage has the same entitlement on their parent's intestacy as a child born within marriage. The same applies to adopted children: s.39 Adoption Act 1976 / s.67 Adoption and Children Act 2002 — an adopted child is treated in law as the child of the adopters and not of the birth parents.

Stepchildren (who have not been adopted) have no automatic entitlement on intestacy — they would need to claim under the Inheritance (Provision for Family and Dependants) Act 1975.
Statutory trusts for issue.summary_line: ILLEGITIMATE CHILDREN · FLRA 1969 · EQUAL RIGHTS · ADOPTED CHILDREN · STEPCHILDREN EXCLUDED · IPFDA 1975

Further relatives and no relatives.front: What is the order of entitlement on intestacy where the deceased leaves no spouse and no issue?
Further relatives and no relatives.summary_line: INTESTACY ORDER · NO SPOUSE NO ISSUE · PARENTS FIRST · SIBLINGS · GRANDPARENTS · UNCLES AUNTS · BONA VACANTIA

Further relatives and no relatives.front: What is bona vacantia and who does the estate pass to if the intestate leaves no qualifying relatives?
Further relatives and no relatives.answer:
Bona vacantia (Latin: 'ownerless goods') applies where an intestate leaves no surviving relatives within the statutory list in s.46 AEA 1925. The estate passes to the Crown (or to the Duchy of Lancaster or Duchy of Cornwall if the intestate was domiciled in those areas).

The Crown has a discretion to make ex gratia payments — for example, to dependants of the intestate who have no legal entitlement, or to close friends. Application is made to the Treasury Solicitor (now the Government Legal Department).

This is an absolute entitlement of the Crown, not a trust or administrative holding.
Further relatives and no relatives.summary_line: BONA VACANTIA · CROWN · DUCHY · NO QUALIFYING RELATIVES · EX GRATIA PAYMENTS · TREASURY SOLICITOR

Further relatives and no relatives.front: Going deeper: The Law Commission has repeatedly called for reform of the intestacy rules to give cohabitants automatic rights on intestacy. Why have these reforms not been implemented, and what does this reveal about the law's treatment of non-traditional family structures?
Further relatives and no relatives.answer:
The current position

Cohabitants — however long the relationship — have no automatic entitlement on intestacy under the Administration of Estates Act 1925. A surviving cohabitant must bring a claim under the IPFDA 1975 within six months of the grant, on the maintenance standard. This is a discretionary remedy, not a right.

The Law Commission's position

The Law Commission's 2011 report on Intestacy and Family Provision proposed that cohabitants who had lived together for five years (or two years where there are children) should receive the same entitlement on intestacy as a surviving spouse. The report described the current position as 'arbitrary and unfair'.

The Cohabitation Rights Bill — a private member's bill — has been introduced in Parliament multiple times (2017, 2019, 2022) without becoming law.

Why reform has stalled

Professor Anne Barlow (Exeter) — a leading empirical researcher on cohabitation law — has identified three obstacles: (1) political reluctance to be seen to undermine marriage as an institution; (2) practical difficulty in defining the point at which cohabitation triggers rights (duration thresholds are arbitrary); (3) opposition from religious and social conservative groups who argue reform incentivises cohabitation over marriage.

The broader picture

Scotland enacted the Family Law (Scotland) Act 2006 giving cohabitants limited but automatic rights on intestacy — a partial reform the Law Commission recommended for England and Wales. The gap between Scottish and English law illustrates the extent to which political choices, rather than legal principle, determine succession rights.

Professor Rebecca Probert (Warwick) has argued that the 'common law marriage myth' — the widespread but legally incorrect belief that long-term cohabitants have the same rights as married couples — makes reform both more urgent and more politically complex.

Partial intestacy.front: What is a partial intestacy and how is it administered?
Partial intestacy.answer:
A partial intestacy arises where a will fails to dispose of the whole estate — for example, where a residuary gift lapses, a beneficiary predeceases, or the will simply makes no provision for part of the estate.

The part not disposed of by the will passes on the intestacy rules under s.46 AEA 1925, as if the testator had died intestate in respect of that property.

PRs administer the estate in two capacities simultaneously: as executors for the property disposed of by the will, and as administrators for the undisposed-of property. The grant of probate covers both — a separate grant of letters of administration is not required.

⚠️ Link — Lapse (see FLK2-WTP-025)
Partial intestacy.summary_line: PARTIAL INTESTACY · WILL FAILS TO DISPOSE · INTESTACY RULES APPLY · TWO CAPACITIES · ONE GRANT

Partial intestacy.front: Where a surviving spouse benefits under a partial intestacy as well as under the will, how does the statutory legacy interact with the testamentary gift?
Partial intestacy.answer:
Under s.49 AEA 1925, where a surviving spouse takes a benefit under the will and also benefits under the intestacy rules (e.g. from an undisposed-of residue), the testamentary benefit is set off against the statutory legacy they would otherwise receive on intestacy.

The spouse does not receive both in full — the statutory legacy is reduced by the value of any interest taken under the will, to prevent double benefit.

This set-off operates on the value of the interest taken under the will. If the testamentary gift exceeds the statutory legacy, the spouse receives nothing additional from the intestate portion beyond what the intestacy rules would otherwise provide for the residue.
Partial intestacy.summary_line: s.49 AEA 1925 · SET-OFF · SPOUSE DOUBLE BENEFIT · TESTAMENTARY GIFT REDUCES STATUTORY LEGACY

Partial intestacy.front: Una's will leaves her car to her friend Vera and her savings account to her nephew Will. Una owns a car, a savings account, and a house. She dies leaving all three assets intact. She has no residuary gift in her will. What happens to the house?
Partial intestacy.answer:
The will disposes of the car (specific gift to Vera) and the savings account (specific gift to Will) but makes no provision for the house or any other property. There is no residuary gift.

This creates a partial intestacy in respect of the house (and any other undisposed-of property): s.33 AEA 1925. The house passes under the intestacy rules as if Una had died intestate in respect of it.

The PRs administer the house under the intestacy rules alongside their executor duties for the specifically gifted assets. No separate grant is required — the grant of probate covers both capacities.

Practical point: the absence of a residuary gift is a common and serious drafting omission. A residuary clause should be included in every will to catch any property not otherwise disposed of.
Partial intestacy.summary_line: PARTIAL INTESTACY · NO RESIDUARY CLAUSE · HOUSE ON INTESTACY · DRAFTING ERROR · TWO CAPACITIES

Partial intestacy.front: Victor's will leaves his house to his wife Wendy and the residue to his two children. Victor also has a substantial pension pot (expression of wishes names his sister as beneficiary) and a joint bank account with Wendy. Victor dies. How is the estate composed for intestacy and distribution purposes?
Partial intestacy.answer:
Assets passing outside the estate (not subject to the will or intestacy):
- Joint bank account: passes to Wendy by survivorship — not part of Victor's estate.
- Pension: paid at the trustees' discretion, guided by the expression of wishes — likely paid to the sister but outside the estate. Not subject to the will or intestacy rules.

Assets within the estate:
- House: passes to Wendy under the will (specific gift). ✓
- Residue: passes to the two children under the will. ✓

There is no partial intestacy on these facts — the will disposes of all estate assets (the house by specific gift, everything else by the residuary clause). The pension and joint account are irrelevant to the testamentary/intestacy analysis.

IHT note: the joint account and pension (if paid to sister) may still be subject to IHT on Victor's death depending on the applicable rules.
Partial intestacy.summary_line: ASSETS OUTSIDE ESTATE · JOINT TENANCY SURVIVORSHIP · PENSION · NOT INTESTACY · IHT NOTE

Spouse + issue calculations.front: Kevin dies intestate survived by his wife Laura and their two adult children. His estate consists of personal chattels worth £20,000, a house worth £400,000, and savings of £180,000. How is the estate distributed?
Spouse + issue calculations.answer:
Total estate: £20,000 (chattels) + £400,000 (house) + £180,000 (savings) = £600,000.

Step 1 — Laura takes all personal chattels: £20,000 absolutely.

Remaining estate: £580,000.

Step 2 — Laura takes statutory legacy: £322,000 absolutely.

Remaining after legacy: £580,000 − £322,000 = £258,000.

Step 3 — Divide remaining £258,000:
- Half to Laura: £129,000 absolutely.
- Half on statutory trusts for issue: £129,000 split equally between the two children = £64,500 each.

Summary: Laura receives £20,000 + £322,000 + £129,000 = £471,000. Each child receives £64,500 (held on trust if under 18, though here they are adults and entitled immediately).
Spouse + issue calculations.summary_line: INTESTACY CALCULATION · SPOUSE + ISSUE · CHATTELS · £322K LEGACY · HALF RESIDUE · AEA 1925

Spouse + issue calculations.front: Mary dies intestate with an estate of £280,000. She is survived by her husband Neil and their daughter Olivia. How is the estate distributed?
Spouse + issue calculations.answer:
The estate (£280,000) is less than the statutory legacy (£322,000).

Where the estate is insufficient to meet the statutory legacy, the surviving spouse takes the entire estate absolutely: s.46 AEA 1925.

Neil takes £280,000 absolutely. Olivia receives nothing — the issue's statutory trust only arises if there is a surplus after the statutory legacy is paid. Since there is no surplus, Olivia has no entitlement on intestacy.

Note: personal chattels are taken first and then set against the statutory legacy calculation. If there are chattels within the £280,000, Neil takes them first and the balance of the estate up to the statutory legacy figure.
Spouse + issue calculations.summary_line: STATUTORY LEGACY · ESTATE BELOW THRESHOLD · SPOUSE TAKES ALL · ISSUE NOTHING · £322K

Spouse + issue calculations.front: Peter dies intestate survived by his civil partner Quinn and three children from a previous relationship (not Quinn's children). Estate: £700,000. Does the relationship between the children and Quinn affect the distribution?
Spouse + issue calculations.answer:
No — the distribution follows the standard intestacy rules regardless of whether the children are Quinn's. The rules apply to the intestate's issue, not to the surviving civil partner's children.

Distribution:
- Quinn takes personal chattels absolutely.
- Quinn takes statutory legacy (£322,000) absolutely.
- Remaining estate: £700,000 − £322,000 − chattels = approximately £378,000 (ignoring chattels value).
- Half of £378,000 (£189,000) → Quinn absolutely.
- Half of £378,000 (£189,000) → statutory trusts for the three children equally: £63,000 each.

The fact that the children are from a previous relationship is legally irrelevant to distribution — they are Peter's issue and entitled in the usual way.
Spouse + issue calculations.summary_line: INTESTACY · PREVIOUS RELATIONSHIP CHILDREN · STANDARD RULES APPLY · CIVIL PARTNER · CALCULATION

No spouse, further relatives.front: Rachel dies intestate with an estate of £150,000. She is not married. She leaves no children. Her mother is alive. She has one full sibling (Sam) and one half-sibling (Tina). Who takes the estate?
No spouse, further relatives.answer:
Apply the intestacy order under s.46 AEA 1925 where there is no spouse and no issue:

1. Parents — Rachel's mother is alive. She takes the entire estate: £150,000 absolutely.

Sam and Tina receive nothing. Siblings (whether full or half blood) only take if there are no surviving parents. The first category in which a relative survives takes the whole estate.
