If Rachel's mother had also predeceased, the estate would pass to siblings of the whole blood (Sam) — and half-blood siblings (Tina) only take if there are no whole-blood siblings.
No spouse, further relatives.summary_line: NO SPOUSE NO ISSUE · PARENT ALIVE · TAKES ALL · SIBLINGS EXCLUDED · s.46 ORDER

No spouse, further relatives.front: Tom dies intestate leaving an estate of £200,000. He is unmarried and has no children, no parents, and no siblings. He has two paternal uncles (whole blood) and one maternal aunt (whole blood). Who inherits?
No spouse, further relatives.answer:
Apply s.46 AEA 1925. With no spouse, issue, parents, or siblings, the estate passes to uncles and aunts of the whole blood (or their issue per stirpes).

All three — the two paternal uncles and the maternal aunt — are uncles/aunts of the whole blood. They share equally: £200,000 ÷ 3 = approximately £66,667 each.

Note: whether they are paternal or maternal is irrelevant — the intestacy rules do not distinguish between paternal and maternal relatives at the same degree. All whole-blood uncles and aunts share equally at this level.
No spouse, further relatives.summary_line: UNCLES AUNTS · WHOLE BLOOD · EQUAL SHARES · PATERNAL MATERNAL IRRELEVANT · s.46

Commorientes on intestacy.front: Xavier (70) and his wife Yvonne (65) are killed in the same accident. It cannot be established who died first. Xavier's estate is £400,000. He dies intestate. How is his estate distributed?
Commorientes on intestacy.answer:
The commorientes presumption applies: s.184 LPA 1925. Where the order of deaths cannot be established, the older person is presumed to have died first.

Xavier (70) is presumed to have died before Yvonne (65).

Consequence: Yvonne is treated as having survived Xavier. She is therefore entitled to Xavier's estate under the intestacy rules as his surviving spouse.

Yvonne then dies (shortly after, on the presumption). Her estate — now including Xavier's £400,000 — passes under Yvonne's own will or intestacy.

Practical risk: this presumption can cause assets to pass to the younger spouse's family rather than the older spouse's family. It is a standard reason to include a survivorship clause (e.g. 30 days) in a will — though that only helps where there is a will.
Commorientes on intestacy.summary_line: COMMORIENTES · s.184 LPA 1925 · OLDER FIRST · YOUNGER INHERITS · SURVIVORSHIP CLAUSE RATIONALE

Lapse and s.33.front: What is lapse and what are its consequences?
Lapse and s.33.answer:
Lapse occurs when a beneficiary predeceases the testator. The gift fails and the subject matter of the gift falls back into the estate.

Consequences:
- A failed specific or general gift falls into residue.
- A failed residuary gift (or share of residue) results in a partial intestacy as to that share.

Exception — s.33 Wills Act 1837: where the gift is to a child or remoter issue of the testator and that beneficiary predeceases leaving issue of their own who survive the testator, the gift does not lapse — it passes to the deceased beneficiary's issue.

Section 33 operates as a statutory substitution. It can be excluded by a contrary intention in the will.

⚠️ Link — Partial intestacy (see FLK2-WTP-024)
Lapse and s.33.summary_line: LAPSE · BENEFICIARY PREDECEASES · FALLS INTO RESIDUE · s.33 EXCEPTION · TESTATOR'S ISSUE ONLY · PARTIAL INTESTACY

Lapse and s.33.front: A testator leaves '£50,000 to my friend Anna'. Anna predeceases the testator, leaving two children. What happens to the £50,000?
Lapse and s.33.answer:
The gift lapses — it fails because Anna predeceased the testator.

Section 33 Wills Act 1837 does not save the gift. Section 33 only applies where the gift is to a child or remoter issue of the testator. Anna is a friend, not issue of the testator — the statutory exception does not apply.

The £50,000 falls into residue (if there is a valid residuary gift) or results in a partial intestacy as to that sum if the residuary gift also fails.

Anna's children have no automatic claim to the gift.
Lapse and s.33.summary_line: LAPSE · FRIEND NOT ISSUE · s.33 NO APPLICATION · FALLS INTO RESIDUE · NO CLAIM BY FRIEND'S CHILDREN

Lapse and s.33.front: Zara's will leaves £30,000 to her daughter Beth and the residue to her son Callum. Beth predeceases Zara, leaving two children (Zara's grandchildren). What happens to the £30,000?
Lapse and s.33.answer:
The gift of £30,000 to Beth does not lapse. Section 33 Wills Act 1837 applies: where a gift is to a child or remoter issue of the testator and that beneficiary predeceases leaving issue of their own who survive the testator, the gift passes to the deceased beneficiary's issue.

Beth is Zara's daughter (issue). Beth predeceased leaving two children (Zara's grandchildren) who survive Zara. Section 33 saves the gift.

The £30,000 passes to Beth's two children equally — £15,000 each. If they are under 18, the sum is held on trust until they reach 18.

The residue passes to Callum as normal. Section 33 only saves the gift — it does not redirect it to Callum or into residue.
Lapse and s.33.summary_line: s.33 · LAPSE SAVED · TESTATOR'S DAUGHTER · GRANDCHILDREN TAKE · PER STIRPES · UNDER 18 TRUST

Lapse and s.33.front: Dan's will leaves his entire estate 'to my children in equal shares'. At the date of the will he has two children: Ed and Flo. Flo predeceases Dan leaving no children. Ed survives. Does Flo's share lapse into residue or pass to Ed?
Lapse and s.33.answer:
This is a class gift to 'my children'. The analysis differs from a specific gift to named individuals.

For class gifts, when a class member fails to qualify (here Flo predeceases with no issue to be substituted under s.33), their share accrues to the surviving class members — it does not fall into residue.

Section 33 does not save Flo's share (she left no issue). However, because this is a class gift, Flo's share accrues to Ed — the sole surviving member of the class.

Ed takes the entire estate. If the gift had been to Ed and Flo as named individuals (not a class gift), Flo's share would fall into residue (or on intestacy if there is no residuary clause). The distinction between class gifts and gifts to named individuals is significant.
Lapse and s.33.summary_line: CLASS GIFT · LAPSE · ACCRUAL TO SURVIVORS · CONTRAST NAMED GIFT · s.33 NO ISSUE

Lapse and s.33.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Maria's will leaves £20,000 to her friend Nina. Nina predeceases Maria, leaving two adult children. Maria's executor asks whether the gift lapses or whether Nina's children inherit the £20,000. A student advises that s.33 Wills Act 1837 saves the gift and it passes to Nina's children.

Is the student correct?
Lapse and s.33.issue: The Trap: the student correctly identifies that s.33 prevents lapse in certain circumstances. The student wrongly applies s.33 to a gift to a friend, missing the key condition that limits the section.
Lapse and s.33.rule: The Reality: section 33 Wills Act 1837 only applies where the gift is to a child or remoter issue of the testator. Nina is Maria's friend — not her child or issue. Section 33 has no application.
Lapse and s.33.application: Why the Distractor Fails: s.33 is a statutory exception to the lapse rule, but it is tightly defined. It saves gifts to the testator's own descendants where those descendants predecease leaving issue of their own. A gift to a friend, colleague, sibling, or any person who is not the testator's issue lapses in the ordinary way if the beneficiary predeceases. Nina's children have no claim. The £20,000 falls into residue (or on intestacy if there is no residuary gift).
Lapse and s.33.conclusion: The Key Rule: s.33 saves lapsed gifts only where the beneficiary is a child or remoter issue of the testator — never for gifts to friends, siblings, or other non-issue beneficiaries.
Lapse and s.33.summary_line: s.33 · ISSUE OF TESTATOR ONLY · FRIEND = NO SAVING · LAPSE · FALLS INTO RESIDUE

Ademption and abatement.front: What is ademption and when does it apply?
Ademption and abatement.answer:
Ademption occurs where the specific subject matter of a specific gift no longer exists or no longer belongs to the testator at the date of death. The gift fails — it is adeemed.

Ademption applies only to specific gifts (gifts of an identifiable asset: 'my car', 'my shares in X plc'). It does not apply to general gifts (a gift of a type of property: '£10,000', '100 shares in any company') or demonstrative gifts.

Examples: the testator sells the car between making the will and dying (adeemed); the testator's house is compulsorily purchased (adeemed, though proceeds may be traced in some circumstances).

The beneficiary receives nothing and has no claim against the estate for the value of the adeemed gift.
Ademption and abatement.summary_line: ADEMPTION · SPECIFIC GIFT · ASSET GONE AT DEATH · BENEFICIARY NOTHING · NO VALUE CLAIM · GENERAL GIFT UNAFFECTED

Ademption and abatement.front: What is abatement and in what order do gifts abate where the estate is insufficient to pay all gifts and debts?
Ademption and abatement.summary_line: ABATEMENT · ORDER · RESIDUE FIRST · GENERAL · DEMONSTRATIVE · SPECIFIC LAST · DEBTS FIRST

Ademption and abatement.front: Maria's will leaves 'my BMW car' to her nephew Nick and the residue to charity. Between making the will and her death, Maria sells the BMW and buys a Mercedes. At death she owns only the Mercedes. What does Nick receive?
Ademption and abatement.answer:
Nick receives nothing. The gift of 'my BMW car' is a specific gift of an identified asset. The BMW no longer belongs to Maria at death — it was sold before she died. The gift is adeemed.

Ademption applies to specific gifts where the subject matter no longer exists or no longer belongs to the testator at death. The sale of the BMW adeems the gift regardless of Maria's intention.

Nick has no claim to the Mercedes — that is a different asset not covered by the gift. Nick has no claim against the estate for the value of the BMW.

The Mercedes falls into residue and passes to charity. If Maria had left 'a car' (a general gift), ademption would not apply — the estate would purchase or provide a car from the general estate.
Ademption and abatement.summary_line: ADEMPTION · SPECIFIC GIFT · BMW SOLD · MERCEDES NOT COVERED · NOTHING TO BENEFICIARY · RESIDUE

Ademption and abatement.front: Oscar's estate is worth £80,000. His will leaves: £50,000 to his sister Pat (general legacy); 'my antique clock' to his friend Quinn (specific gift); and the residue to charity. His debts and funeral expenses total £40,000. How are the gifts satisfied?
Ademption and abatement.answer:
Step 1 — Pay debts and expenses: £40,000. Remaining estate: £80,000 − £40,000 = £40,000.

Step 2 — Apply abatement. The order (Sch.1 AEA 1925) is:
1. Residue abates first — charity's residuary gift bears the shortfall.
2. General legacies abate next — Pat's £50,000.
3. Specific gifts abate last — Quinn's clock.

Residuary estate: after paying specific gift (clock, say worth £5,000) and general legacy (£50,000), residue would be £40,000 − £5,000 − £50,000 = negative. So residue is exhausted first (charity takes nothing).

Remaining after residue exhausted: £40,000. Deduct specific gift (clock, £5,000): £35,000 available for general legacy. Pat's £50,000 abates to £35,000. Quinn takes the clock in full.

Summary: Quinn — clock; Pat — £35,000; charity — nothing.
Ademption and abatement.summary_line: ABATEMENT · DEBTS FIRST · RESIDUE EXHAUSTED · GENERAL ABATES · SPECIFIC LAST · CALCULATION

Types of gift.front: Distinguish between specific, general, demonstrative and residuary gifts.
Types of gift.answer:
Specific gift: a gift of a particular identified asset owned by the testator ('my BMW', 'my shares in Tesco plc'). Subject to ademption. Abates last.

General gift: a gift of a type of property, not identified as a specific asset ('£10,000', '100 shares in any company'). Satisfied from the general estate. Not subject to ademption.

Demonstrative gift: a gift of a general amount but directed to be satisfied from a specific fund ('£10,000 from my Barclays account'). If the fund is insufficient, the shortfall is met from the general estate — so it is not fully subject to ademption. Abates after general gifts.

Residuary gift: a gift of whatever remains after all specific, general and demonstrative gifts, debts, and expenses have been satisfied. Abates first.
Types of gift.summary_line: TYPES OF GIFT · SPECIFIC · GENERAL · DEMONSTRATIVE · RESIDUARY · ABATEMENT ORDER · ADEMPTION

Types of gift.front: The testator leaves 'my gold watch to A, £5,000 from my NatWest savings account to B, and the rest of my estate to C'. Classify each gift and explain the significance.
Types of gift.answer:
Gift to A ('my gold watch'): specific gift. It is an identified, owned asset. If the watch no longer exists at death, the gift is adeemed — A receives nothing. A's gift abates last.

Gift to B ('£5,000 from my NatWest savings account'): demonstrative gift. It is a pecuniary gift directed from a specific fund. If the NatWest account has insufficient funds, the shortfall is met from the general estate — so it is not fully adeemed if the account is empty. B's gift abates after general gifts.

Gift to C ('the rest of my estate'): residuary gift. C takes whatever is left after debts, expenses, and the prior gifts. C's gift abates first and may be exhausted entirely if the estate is insufficient.
Types of gift.summary_line: GIFT CLASSIFICATION · SPECIFIC WATCH · DEMONSTRATIVE BANK ACCOUNT · RESIDUE · ABATEMENT CONSEQUENCES

Commorientes and class gifts.front: What is the commorientes rule and how does it apply on testacy and intestacy?
Commorientes and class gifts.answer:
The commorientes rule (s.184 Law of Property Act 1925) applies where two or more people die in circumstances where the order of death cannot be determined. The law presumes they died in order of seniority — the older person is presumed to have died first.

On testacy: if a beneficiary is presumed to have survived the testator (being younger), they are treated as having inherited — the estate then passes under the beneficiary's own will or intestacy.

On intestacy: the same presumption applies. The practical consequence is that a younger spouse is presumed to survive an older spouse, potentially inheriting the estate which then passes to the younger spouse's relatives.

Wills often include a survivorship condition (e.g. '28 days') to avoid the commorientes rule producing unintended results.
Commorientes and class gifts.summary_line: COMMORIENTES · s.184 LPA 1925 · OLDER DIES FIRST · SURVIVORSHIP CLAUSE · INTESTACY CONSEQUENCES

Commorientes and class gifts.front: What is a class gift, when does the class close, and what is the significance of the closing?
Commorientes and class gifts.answer:
A class gift is a gift to a group defined by a shared characteristic ('to my grandchildren', 'to my children who attain 21'). The members of the class share the gift equally.

The class closes when no further members can be added. Rules of convenience determine the closing date:
- If any member of the class satisfies the condition at the testator's death, the class closes at death — later-born members are excluded.
- If no member satisfies the condition at death, the class remains open until the first member satisfies it — the class closes at that point.

Significance: once the class closes, later-born individuals cannot enter — even if they would have qualified. This prevents the estate being held on trust indefinitely awaiting potential new members. The shares of class members who fail to qualify are redistributed among those who do.
Commorientes and class gifts.summary_line: CLASS GIFT · CLOSING RULES · MEMBER QUALIFIES AT DEATH · LATER BORN EXCLUDED · ACCRUAL · SHARES REDISTRIBUTE

Gift classification.front: Rita's will leaves '£20,000 from my HSBC savings account to my nephew Sam'. At Rita's death the HSBC account contains only £8,000. What does Sam receive and why?
Gift classification.answer:
This is a demonstrative gift — a gift of a general amount directed to be satisfied from a specific fund.

A demonstrative gift is not subject to full ademption. Where the specified fund is insufficient to satisfy the full gift, the shortfall is met from the general estate.

Sam receives £20,000 in total: £8,000 from the HSBC account and £12,000 from the general estate.

Contrast with a specific gift: if the gift had been 'the balance of my HSBC savings account', that would be a specific gift — Sam would receive only £8,000 (the balance) with no right to claim the shortfall from the general estate.

Demonstrative gifts occupy an intermediate position — they benefit from the general estate if the fund is insufficient, but are satisfied from the named fund first.
Gift classification.summary_line: DEMONSTRATIVE GIFT · FUND INSUFFICIENT · GENERAL ESTATE TOPS UP · CONTRAST SPECIFIC · £20K PAID

Witnessing and s.15.front: What is the effect of a witness or their spouse/civil partner being a beneficiary under the will?
Witnessing and s.15.answer:
Under s.15 Wills Act 1837, a gift to a witness (or to the spouse or civil partner of a witness at the time of execution) is void. The will itself remains valid and is admitted to probate — only the gift fails.

The witness still counts for the purpose of s.9 attestation — the will is not invalidated by the conflict. The effect is that the witness loses their benefit but their attestation is still valid.

If there are only two witnesses and one is a beneficiary (or their spouse is), the gift to that person fails absolutely. There is no saving provision.

Note: the relevant relationship is at the time of execution — a witness who marries a beneficiary after execution is not affected.
Witnessing and s.15.summary_line: s.15 WILLS ACT · WITNESS SPOUSE · GIFT VOID · WILL VALID · ATTESTATION COUNTS · TIME OF EXECUTION

Witnessing and s.15.front: The testator's will leaves the residue to their spouse. The spouse witnesses the will. What are the consequences?
Witnessing and s.15.answer:
The gift of residue to the spouse is void under s.15 Wills Act 1837. The spouse is a witness and a beneficiary — the gift fails absolutely.

The will itself is not invalidated — it is admitted to probate. The attestation counts for the purpose of s.9.

Consequence for the estate: if the residue fails, there is a total or partial intestacy in respect of the residue. On intestacy, the spouse may still be entitled to a share of the estate under the AEA 1925 intestacy rules — but they have lost the testamentary gift.

This is a common and serious drafting error. The solicitor who prepared the will without advising the client to use independent witnesses may be liable in negligence: White v Jones [1995].
Witnessing and s.15.summary_line: s.15 · SPOUSE WITNESSES · GIFT TO SPOUSE VOID · RESIDUE ABSORBS · WHITE V JONES · SOLICITOR NEGLIGENCE

Witnessing and s.15.front: Gina's will leaves £10,000 to her friend Harry and the residue to her sister Iris. Harry's wife, Jane, witnesses the will alongside a neighbour. Is the gift to Harry valid?
Witnessing and s.15.answer:
No. Section 15 Wills Act 1837 voids a gift to the spouse or civil partner of a witness. Jane (Harry's wife) witnessed the will. Harry is the spouse of a witness.

The gift of £10,000 to Harry is void — it fails absolutely. Harry receives nothing.

The will itself is valid and is admitted to probate — s.15 does not invalidate the will, only the gift to the witness's spouse. Jane's attestation counts for the purpose of s.9 formalities.

The £10,000 falls into residue (unless it creates a partial intestacy). Iris takes the residue — which now includes the £10,000 — under the residuary gift.
Witnessing and s.15.summary_line: s.15 · WITNESS SPOUSE · GIFT VOID · WILL VALID · ATTESTATION COUNTS · FALLS INTO RESIDUE

Witnessing and s.15.front: Ken witnesses a will in which his civil partner Leo is the sole residuary beneficiary. After execution but before the testator's death, Ken and Leo dissolve their civil partnership. Leo survives the testator. Does Leo receive the residue?
Witnessing and s.15.answer:
Yes — Leo's gift is valid. Section 15 Wills Act 1837 voids a gift to the spouse or civil partner of a witness. The relevant relationship is assessed at the time of execution — not at the date of the testator's death.

At the time of execution, Leo was Ken's civil partner — s.15 would have voided the gift at that point.

However: the civil partnership was subsequently dissolved. By the time of the testator's death, Leo is no longer Ken's civil partner. The question is therefore whether s.15 applies based on the position at execution or at death.

The answer is at execution: s.15 renders the gift void if the civil partnership existed at execution. Since it did exist at execution, the gift to Leo is technically void under s.15.

Note: this is a nuanced point — the section is engaged at execution. The subsequent dissolution does not cure the defect.
Witnessing and s.15.summary_line: s.15 · TIME OF EXECUTION · SUBSEQUENT DISSOLUTION · STILL VOID · RELATIONSHIP AT EXECUTION GOVERNS

Grant of representation.front: What is the difference between a grant of probate and letters of administration?
Grant of representation.answer:
Grant of probate: issued by the Probate Registry to the executor(s) named in a valid will. It confirms the executor's authority to administer the estate and the will's validity. An executor's authority derives from the will — they can act from the moment of death, even before the grant issues.

Letters of administration: issued where there is no valid will (intestacy), no executor willing or able to act, or where the executor has died or renounced. The court appoints an administrator. An administrator has no authority to act until the grant issues.

Letters of administration with will annexed (c.t.a.): issued where there is a will but no executor able to act — the named will is administered, but by a court-appointed administrator.
Grant of representation.summary_line: GRANT OF REPRESENTATION · PROBATE VS LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION · EXECUTOR FROM WILL · ADMINISTRATOR FROM COURT

Grant of representation.front: Who is entitled to a grant of letters of administration on intestacy, and in what order?
Grant of representation.summary_line: LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION · r.22 NCPR 1987 · ORDER OF ENTITLEMENT · SPOUSE FIRST · ISSUE · PARENTS

Grant of representation.front: Is a grant of representation always required, and what assets pass outside the estate?
Grant of representation.answer:
A grant of representation is not always required. Assets that pass outside the estate do not require probate and are not administered by the PRs:

1. Joint tenancy property — passes by survivorship to the surviving co-owner automatically on death.
2. Life insurance proceeds — paid directly to the named beneficiary under the policy (or to the deceased's estate if no beneficiary is named).
3. Pension death benefits — expression of wishes directs payment at the trustees' discretion; usually paid without a grant.
4. Small estates — some financial institutions will release small sums without a grant under their own thresholds.

For assets held in the deceased's sole name above the relevant threshold, a grant is required. This is a practical point with significant IHT implications — assets passing by survivorship may still be subject to IHT.
Grant of representation.summary_line: GRANT NOT ALWAYS REQUIRED · JOINT TENANCY SURVIVORSHIP · LIFE INSURANCE · PENSION · SMALL ESTATES

Grant of representation.front: Tim dies leaving a valid will appointing his sister Uma as executor. Uma is willing to act. Tim's estate includes a house in his sole name, a bank account, and a portfolio of shares. Uma asks whether she needs to obtain probate before she can deal with the assets. Advise.
Grant of representation.answer:
Uma should apply for a grant of probate. Although an executor's authority derives from the will (not the grant), most third parties — banks, share registrars, HMLR — will not deal with an executor without sight of the grant.

Practically: Uma cannot register the transfer of the house without producing the grant to HMLR. Banks will not release funds above their internal thresholds without probate. Share registrars require the grant to transfer or sell shares.

Exceptions (assets Uma can deal with without a grant): she can take custody of personal chattels, arrange the funeral, and take steps to preserve estate assets. She can also receive small sums where institutions agree.

Conclusion: Uma must apply for a grant of probate at the Probate Registry. She should simultaneously file the IHT account (IHT400 or IHT205 for excepted estates) with HMRC, as IHT must be paid before the grant issues.
Grant of representation.summary_line: PROBATE REQUIRED · THIRD PARTY INSTITUTIONS · HMLR · BANK · GRANT NEEDED IN PRACTICE

Grant of representation.front: Vera dies intestate. Her husband Wade and her adult daughter Xena both want to administer the estate. Wade says he has priority. Is he right, and how many administrators are required?
Grant of representation.answer:
Wade is correct that he has priority. Under r.22 Non-Contentious Probate Rules 1987, the surviving spouse or civil partner has the first entitlement to apply for letters of administration on intestacy — ahead of children.

Wade may apply alone as the sole administrator. There is no general requirement for two administrators — one administrator suffices where there are no minor beneficiaries and no life interests in the estate.

Exception: if any beneficiary is a minor (under 18) or if there is a life interest in the estate, a minimum of two administrators (or a trust corporation) is required. On the facts, Xena is an adult — but if there were minor grandchildren with an interest, two administrators would be needed.

Xena may apply together with Wade if both are willing — the grant can be taken jointly. But she has no right to insist on being appointed if Wade applies alone.
Grant of representation.summary_line: LETTERS OF ADMINISTRATION · SPOUSE PRIORITY · r.22 NCPR · ONE ADMINISTRATOR SUFFICIENT · MINOR EXCEPTION

Grant of representation.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

Tina dies leaving a valid will appointing her sister Uma as sole executor. Uma wants to access Tina's bank account immediately to pay the funeral expenses. She presents the will to the bank. The bank refuses to release funds without a grant of probate. Uma's solicitor tells her the bank is wrong — an executor's authority comes from the will, not the grant, so Uma can demand access immediately.

Is the solicitor right?
Grant of representation.issue: The Trap: the solicitor correctly states the legal principle — an executor's authority does derive from the will, not the grant. The solicitor then wrongly concludes that this entitles Uma to compel the bank to release funds without a grant.
Grant of representation.rule: The Reality: while an executor's authority is legally derived from the will (and technically exists from the moment of death), third parties — including banks, share registrars, and HMLR — are not obliged to accept the will alone as proof of authority. They may lawfully require production of a grant of probate before releasing assets.
Grant of representation.application: Why the Distractor Fails: the legal principle and the practical position are different things. The bank is not 'wrong' — it is entitled to require a grant for its own protection. A bank that releases funds without a grant risks liability if the will is later challenged or proved invalid. Uma's legal authority exists but cannot be enforced against the bank without the grant. The solicitor should advise Uma to apply for probate promptly. In the interim, many banks will release modest sums for funeral expenses as a matter of practice — but they are not legally required to.
Grant of representation.conclusion: The Key Rule: an executor's authority derives from the will — but third parties may lawfully require a grant of probate before acting. Legal authority and practical enforceability are not the same thing.
Grant of representation.summary_line: EXECUTOR · AUTHORITY FROM WILL · GRANT REQUIRED BY THIRD PARTIES · LEGAL VS PRACTICAL AUTHORITY

Personal representatives — duties and po.front: What are the core duties of a personal representative in administering an estate?
Personal representatives — duties and po.answer:
Personal representatives (PRs) — executors or administrators — owe the following core duties:

1. Collect and get in estate assets — all assets belonging to the deceased at death.
2. Pay funeral expenses, testamentary expenses, and debts in the correct order of priority.
3. Pay legacies and distribute the residue to those entitled.
4. File an IHT account with HMRC (IHT400 or IHT205 for excepted estates) and pay any IHT due before probate issues.
5. Preserve estate assets during administration — invest prudently; avoid conflict of interest.
6. Keep accounts — maintain accurate records of assets collected and liabilities discharged.
7. Administer within a reasonable time — the executor's year (one year from death) sets the expectation, though complex estates take longer.

PRs are in a fiduciary position and must not profit from their role unless authorised.
Personal representatives — duties and po.summary_line: PR DUTIES · COLLECT ASSETS · PAY DEBTS · DISTRIBUTE · IHT ACCOUNT · PRESERVE · EXECUTOR'S YEAR · FIDUCIARY

Personal representatives — duties and po.front: What is the executor's year and what are its consequences?
Personal representatives — duties and po.answer:
The executor's year is the principle that beneficiaries cannot compel a PR to distribute the estate within one year of the testator's death. The PR has a reasonable period — conventionally one year — to collect assets, pay debts, and prepare for distribution.

Consequences:
- Beneficiaries cannot bring a claim for immediate distribution within the executor's year, even if they are absolutely entitled.
- After one year, a PR who fails to distribute without reasonable cause may be in breach of duty and liable to pay interest on pecuniary legacies (currently at the rate set by the court — 6% per annum on legacies since 2020).
- A residuary beneficiary may seek an account from the PRs after one year.

The year runs from the date of death, not from the grant of representation.
Personal representatives — duties and po.summary_line: EXECUTOR'S YEAR · ONE YEAR FROM DEATH · NO COMPULSION TO DISTRIBUTE · INTEREST ON LEGACIES AFTER · 6%

Personal representatives — duties and po.front: Can a sole executor appoint another person to act in their place, and what is the rule in Re Earl of Chesterfield's Trusts?
Personal representatives — duties and po.answer:
A sole executor has no power to appoint a substitute executor — the power of an executor derives from the will or the court, not from delegation. A sole executor can, however, appoint an attorney to act on their behalf under a lasting or enduring power of attorney for administrative acts.

Re Earl of Chesterfield's Trusts (1883) is relevant to a different but connected point — the conversion rule for mixed trust estates containing wasted and reversionary interests. In the administration context, the principle most relevant to executors acting alone is that all executors must act unanimously in respect of trust property (unlike in simple administration where any executor can act alone).

For estate administration (as opposed to trust management): a single executor can act alone in collecting assets and paying debts. For trustee functions following the executor's year, unanimity is required.
Personal representatives — duties and po.summary_line: SOLE EXECUTOR · NO SUBSTITUTION · UNANIMITY FOR TRUST · SINGLE EXECUTOR FOR ADMINISTRATION

Personal representatives — duties and po.issue: Does a solicitor owe a duty of care to intended beneficiaries under a will they negligently fail to prepare in time?
Personal representatives — duties and po.rule:
A solicitor instructed to prepare a will owes a duty of care not only to the testator client but also to the intended beneficiaries. Where the solicitor's negligence causes the intended beneficiary to lose their legacy (e.g. because the testator dies before the will is executed), the beneficiary has a claim in tort against the solicitor. The duty is imposed as an incremental extension of Hedley Byrne — the solicitor has assumed responsibility to the beneficiaries.

⚠️ Link — Negligence / Hedley Byrne / solicitor's duty (see FLK1-TORT — White v Jones card)
Personal representatives — duties and po.application: The testator instructed his solicitors to prepare a new will reinstating his daughters as beneficiaries after a family dispute. The solicitors delayed unreasonably. The testator died before the new will was executed. The daughters received nothing under the existing will.
Personal representatives — duties and po.conclusion: The daughters succeeded against the solicitors in negligence. White v Jones is authority for the extended duty of care owed by a solicitor to intended beneficiaries. It has direct relevance to WTP: solicitors preparing wills must act promptly — delay causing a beneficiary to lose their legacy can ground a claim even though the beneficiary had no contract with the firm.
Personal representatives — duties and po.summary_line: WHITE V JONES · SOLICITOR DUTY · INTENDED BENEFICIARIES · ASSUMED RESPONSIBILITY · NEGLIGENCE · DELAY · HEDLEY BYRNE

Collection and realisation of assets.front: What is an assent and when is it required?
Collection and realisation of assets.answer:
An assent is the formal act by which a PR transfers a specific asset to a beneficiary entitled under the will or on intestacy. It is the mechanism by which legal title vests in the beneficiary.

When required: an assent is required for all property that vests in the PR by virtue of the grant — i.e. all assets in the deceased's sole name. Without an assent, legal title remains with the PRs.

Formalities:
- Land: an assent of a legal estate in land must be in writing, signed by the PR, and state the name of the person in whose favour it is made: s.36 AEA 1925. For registered land, the assent must be registered at HMLR to transfer the legal estate.
- Personal property: an assent of personal property requires no particular formality — it can be oral or by conduct, though written assents are good practice.
Collection and realisation of assets.summary_line: ASSENT · s.36 AEA 1925 · IN WRITING · SIGNED BY PR · LEGAL ESTATE · REGISTERED LAND REGISTRATION NEEDED

Collection and realisation of assets.front: What practical steps must a PR take when collecting and realising estate assets?
Collection and realisation of assets.answer:
PRs should take the following practical steps:

1. Identify all assets — review bank statements, investment portfolios, property registers, insurance policies, pension nominations.
2. Value all assets at the date of death — required for IHT purposes and to calculate the estate.
3. Notify institutions of the death and produce the grant of representation to access accounts.
4. Realise (sell) assets where necessary to pay debts, IHT, or to make distributions in cash.
5. Register the grant — for land, register the assent or transfer at HMLR.
6. Deal with jointly owned assets — these pass by survivorship and do not vest in the PRs.
7. Collect income accrued but not received at death — e.g. rent, dividends declared.
8. Obtain a clearance certificate from HMRC (IHT30) once IHT is settled, protecting PRs from further claims.
Collection and realisation of assets.summary_line: PR PRACTICAL STEPS · VALUE AT DEATH · NOTIFY INSTITUTIONS · REALISE ASSETS · IHT30 CLEARANCE

Collection and realisation of assets.issue: Where a beneficiary cannot be found after reasonable enquiry, can the PRs distribute the estate without locating them?
Collection and realisation of assets.rule: Where a beneficiary's existence or whereabouts cannot be established after all reasonable enquiries, the court may make a 'Benjamin order' — an order permitting PRs to distribute the estate on the basis that the missing beneficiary has predeceased or is otherwise not entitled. PRs who distribute pursuant to such an order are protected from personal liability.
Collection and realisation of assets.application: The son of the testator could not be traced. After extensive enquiries, the court permitted distribution on the footing that he had predeceased his father. If the son later appeared, his remedy would lie against the beneficiaries who received his share — not against the PRs.
Collection and realisation of assets.conclusion: A Benjamin order was granted. Re Benjamin is the authority for distribution in the face of a missing beneficiary — essential protection for PRs administering an estate where enquiries have been exhausted. Alternatively, PRs may take out missing beneficiary insurance.
Collection and realisation of assets.summary_line: RE BENJAMIN · MISSING BENEFICIARY · COURT ORDER · DISTRIBUTION PERMITTED · PR PROTECTION · INSURANCE

Collection and realisation of assets.front: Going deeper: Existing succession law was designed for a world of tangible assets and registered accounts. How does it cope — or fail to cope — with digital assets such as cryptocurrency, online accounts, and digital intellectual property?
Collection and realisation of assets.answer:
The problem

Digital assets do not fit neatly into traditional succession categories:

1. Cryptocurrency: Bitcoin and similar assets are property (AA v Persons Unknown [2019] confirmed this in the injunction context), but they are held by private key — if the testator dies without disclosing the key, the assets are permanently lost. There is no registry, no institution to contact, no administrator who can release the assets. Standard administration processes are useless.

2. Online accounts: most platform terms of service (Apple, Google, Facebook) prohibit transfer of accounts on death — the account is personal and non-transferable. A PR who tries to access a deceased's account may breach the Computer Misuse Act 1990, even with the deceased's password.

3. Digital intellectual property: copyright in original works (photographs, writing, code) survives death and passes to the estate — but non-fungible tokens (NFTs) and platform-specific digital goods (in-game items, streaming libraries) may not be transferable at all under the platform's terms.

The legal gap

Professor Richard Frimston (Society of Trust and Estate Practitioners) has argued that English succession law has no mechanism to:
- Compel platforms to disclose account contents to PRs;
- Guarantee PRs lawful access to digital assets without platform cooperation;
- Treat cryptocurrency as a standard estate asset given its keyless nature.

The Law Commission's 2023 consultation paper on Digital Assets acknowledged the problem in the property context but did not address succession directly.

Practical responses

The only effective current solutions are testamentary — leaving a memorandum of digital assets and access credentials (not in the will itself, which becomes public on probate), or using a digital asset vault service. The Society of Trust and Estate Practitioners recommends a digital asset audit as part of will-drafting. This is a practical workaround for a legal framework that has not kept pace with technology.

Payment of debts — order of application.subtopic: Payment of debts — order of application
Payment of debts — order of application.front: In what order must a PR pay the debts of a solvent estate?
Payment of debts — order of application.answer:
A PR must first pay:
1. Funeral and testamentary expenses (costs of obtaining the grant, valuation fees, etc.);
2. Debts and liabilities of the deceased;
3. IHT attributable to the estate.

Only after all debts are discharged does the PR proceed to pay legacies and distribute the residue.

For a solvent estate, the order in which specific assets are used to pay debts depends on the statutory order of application in Schedule 1 AEA 1925 (as modified by any directions in the will). Residue is used first, then property subject to a general legacy, then specific gifts last.

⚠️ Link — Abatement (see FLK2-WTP-031)
Payment of debts — order of application.summary_line: SOLVENT ESTATE · DEBTS FIRST · FUNERAL EXPENSES · TESTAMENTARY EXPENSES · THEN LEGACIES · ABATEMENT

Payment of debts — order of application.subtopic: Payment of debts — order of application
Payment of debts — order of application.front: In what order are assets applied to pay debts in an insolvent estate?
Payment of debts — order of application.summary_line: INSOLVENT ESTATE · INSOLVENCY ACT 1986 · SECURED · FUNERAL · PREFERENTIAL · UNSECURED PARI PASSU · BENEFICIARIES NOTHING

Payment of debts — order of application.subtopic: Payment of debts — order of application
Payment of debts — order of application.front: What is the statutory order in which assets are used to pay debts in a solvent estate where the will makes no specific provision?
Payment of debts — order of application.summary_line: STATUTORY ORDER · SCHEDULE 1 AEA 1925 · RESIDUE FIRST · GENERAL LEGACIES · SPECIFIC LAST · WILL CAN ALTER

Payment of debts — order of application.subtopic: Payment of debts — order of application
Payment of debts — order of application.issue: Where PRs wrongly distribute assets — paying a charity instead of the lawful beneficiaries — what remedies are available and against whom?
Payment of debts — order of application.rule: PRs who distribute an estate incorrectly (devastavit) are personally liable to the prejudiced beneficiaries. Additionally, the prejudiced beneficiary has a personal claim — not a proprietary tracing claim — against the recipients of the wrongly distributed assets, who are liable to restore the value received, regardless of fault. The right is restitutionary.
Payment of debts — order of application.application: The testator's will was invalid. The PRs, unaware, distributed the estate to charities named in the will instead of the next of kin entitled on intestacy. The next of kin brought personal claims against the charities to recover the sums paid.
Payment of debts — order of application.conclusion: The claims succeeded. Ministry of Health v Simpson establishes the personal liability of recipients of wrongly distributed estates to make restitution, even as innocent recipients. It is a foundational case in the law of unjust enrichment as applied to estates.
Payment of debts — order of application.summary_line: MINISTRY OF HEALTH V SIMPSON · WRONGFUL DISTRIBUTION · PERSONAL CLAIM · RESTITUTION · INNOCENT RECIPIENT · DEVASTAVIT

Payment of debts — order of application.subtopic: Payment of debts — order of application
Payment of debts — order of application.front: Going deeper: Ministry of Health v Simpson [1951] established a personal (not proprietary) remedy against recipients of wrongly distributed estates. Why does this distinction matter and is a proprietary remedy available in any circumstances?
Payment of debts — order of application.answer:
The distinction in outline

A personal remedy requires the defendant to pay the claimant a sum equal to the unjust enrichment — but gives no priority over the defendant's other creditors if they are insolvent. A proprietary remedy gives the claimant a right over a specific asset — it survives the defendant's insolvency and takes priority.

Ministry of Health v Simpson: the personal remedy

The House of Lords held that a beneficiary whose interest was defeated by wrongful distribution has a personal claim in equity (not in common law) against the recipient, without proof of fault. The liability is restitutionary — the recipient must restore the value received. This is not a tracing claim — the beneficiary need not identify specific assets received by the defendant.

Professor Peter Birks (Unjust Enrichment) argued that this personal remedy is best understood as a claim for unjust enrichment — the ground of unjust enrichment is the failure of the basis on which the payment was made (the distribution was legally unauthorised).

The case for a proprietary remedy

Equity recognises proprietary claims where there is a fiduciary relationship or a specific identifiable asset that can be traced. Where the wrongly distributed asset can be traced in the hands of the recipient (e.g. it has been invested in an identifiable fund), a constructive trust or equitable charge may be imposed: Foskett v McKeown [2001].

However, Ministry of Health v Simpson did not establish a general proprietary remedy — it confirmed only the personal claim. This leaves beneficiaries vulnerable where the recipient has dissipated the assets or become insolvent.

Professor Graham Virgo (Cambridge, Principles of the Law of Restitution) has argued that the law should recognise a proprietary remedy in wrongful distribution cases wherever the asset is traceable — but English courts have not yet taken this step. The personal remedy remains the primary tool, with proprietary claims reserved for cases of specific traceability.

Assents.front: What protection does a purchaser have where land has previously been the subject of an assent?
Assents.answer:
Under s.36(6) AEA 1925, a purchaser (including a mortgage lender) is protected if they obtain from the PR a written statement that the PR has not made a previous assent or conveyance of the same legal estate.

If the PR gives such a statement fraudulently, the purchaser is still protected — they do not need to investigate further.

For registered land: once a transfer or assent is registered at HMLR, the register entry provides conclusive evidence of the current legal owner. A purchaser of registered land is protected by the register.

For unregistered land: the s.36(6) endorsement on the grant of representation is the protective mechanism — a purchaser taking the endorsed grant takes free of any prior unregistered assent.
Assents.summary_line: s.36(6) AEA 1925 · PRIOR ASSENT · WRITTEN STATEMENT · PURCHASER PROTECTION · REGISTERED LAND REGISTER

Assents.front: A PR assents to a property in favour of the residuary beneficiary. Six months later, a creditor surfaces. Can the PR recover the property?
Assents.answer:
Potentially yes. A PR who assents prematurely — before paying all debts — may recover the property from the beneficiary if the creditor's claim means the estate is insufficient without it, provided the beneficiary has not given consideration.

Under s.36(8) AEA 1925, where a PR has mistakenly assented to property before paying all debts, the PR may re-vest the property to meet outstanding liabilities if the beneficiary has not given value and took with notice of the circumstances.

A purchaser for value from the beneficiary (without notice) would take free and could not be disturbed. The PR's personal liability to the creditor would then arise instead.

This risk underlines why PRs should not distribute until satisfied all creditors have been paid — commonly after six months from the grant (the limitation period for creditor claims under the Limitation Act 1980 does not run from death, but PRs typically wait).
Assents.summary_line: PREMATURE ASSENT · PR RECOVERY · s.36(8) · NO CONSIDERATION · CREDITOR EMERGED · PURCHASER PROTECTED

Assents.front: Eve is executor of her father's estate. His will leaves the family home to her brother Fred. The house is registered land. Eve prepares an assent in writing, signs it, and hands it to Fred. Fred does not register it. Three months later, Eve — who has now distributed the rest of the estate — realises a creditor has emerged with a claim of £30,000. Can Eve recover the house?
Assents.answer:
Potentially yes, but with complications.

The assent was made in writing and signed by Eve (satisfying s.36 AEA 1925 for land). However, the legal estate in registered land only passes to Fred on registration at HMLR — until registration, Fred holds an equitable interest only (s.27 LRA 2002). The legal estate remains with Eve as PR.

Eve may seek to recover the property to meet the creditor's claim under s.36(8) AEA 1925 — an executor who assents in error before all debts are paid may recover the property from a beneficiary who has not given value.

However: Fred has the assent and an equitable interest. If Fred registers before Eve acts, Eve loses the ability to recover the legal estate. Eve should act immediately to register a restriction or protect her position.

Risk to Eve: if Fred has already registered and sold to a third party purchaser for value without notice, Eve cannot recover the property and is personally liable to the creditor.
Assents.summary_line: PREMATURE ASSENT · s.36(8) · RECOVERY POSSIBLE · EQUITABLE INTEREST · REGISTRATION RISK

Assents.front: Guy is the executor of an unregistered freehold estate. He assents the property to himself as the sole beneficiary. A later purchaser, Harriet, investigates title and finds no endorsement on the grant of probate confirming no previous assent has been made. Should Harriet be concerned?
Assents.answer:
Yes — Harriet has a legitimate concern. Under s.36(6) AEA 1925, a purchaser of unregistered land is protected against a previous unregistered assent if they obtain a written statement from the PR that no previous assent or conveyance has been made of the same legal estate.

The protection mechanism is an endorsement on the grant of probate (or a separate written statement). Without it, Harriet cannot confirm that Guy's assent to himself was the only disposition — there could in theory be an earlier undisclosed assent.

Practical advice to Harriet: require Guy (or his solicitors) to provide the s.36(6) written statement confirming no prior assent. If Guy cannot or will not provide this, Harriet should not proceed without title indemnity insurance.

For registered land, this issue does not arise — HMLR registration provides conclusive evidence of the current owner.
Assents.summary_line: s.36(6) · PRIOR ASSENT ENDORSEMENT · UNREGISTERED LAND · PURCHASER PROTECTION · INSURANCE

Post-death variations.front: What is a deed of variation and what are the conditions for it to be effective for IHT and CGT purposes?
Post-death variations.summary_line: DEED OF VARIATION · s.142 IHTA 1984 · TWO YEARS · IN WRITING · NO CONSIDERATION · IHT TREATED AS DECEASED · CGT s.62

Post-death variations.front: A testator leaves her entire estate to her adult daughter. The estate includes a house worth £400,000. The daughter wants to redirect the house to her own children (the testator's grandchildren) to save IHT on her own future death. She does so by deed of variation within two years. What are the IHT consequences?
Post-death variations.answer:
The deed of variation redirects the house from the daughter to the grandchildren within two years of death and includes the s.142 IHTA 1984 election.

IHT consequences on the testator's estate: the variation is treated as if the testator had left the house directly to the grandchildren. The testator's estate is recalculated on that basis. If the grandchildren are direct descendants, the RNRB may be available (the house passing to direct descendants ✓).

IHT consequences for the daughter: because the s.142 election applies, the variation is not treated as a gift from the daughter to the grandchildren. It is not a PET or CLT — the daughter's seven-year clock does not start running. She gives up the house with no IHT consequences to herself.

Key limit: no consideration can pass from the grandchildren to the daughter. If the daughter receives anything in exchange, s.142 does not apply and the transfer is a PET by the daughter.
Post-death variations.summary_line: DEED OF VARIATION · REDIRECT TO GRANDCHILDREN · RNRB · NO PET BY DAUGHTER · NO CONSIDERATION CONDITION

Post-death variations.front: What is a disclaimer in the context of succession and how does it differ from a deed of variation?
Post-death variations.answer:
A disclaimer is a beneficiary's refusal to accept an interest under a will or on intestacy. A beneficiary who disclaims takes nothing — they cannot direct the disclaimed interest to a third party of their choice.

Effect: the disclaimed interest passes as if the disclaiming beneficiary had predeceased the testator — it falls into residue or passes on intestacy accordingly.

Key distinction from a deed of variation:
- Deed of variation: the beneficiary accepts the interest and then redirects it to a specified third party. The beneficiary chooses who receives the asset.
- Disclaimer: the beneficiary refuses the interest entirely. They have no control over where it goes — it follows the will or intestacy rules.

IHT: a disclaimer is also treated as made by the deceased under s.93 IHTA 1984 (within two years) — no IHT consequences for the disclaiming beneficiary, provided no benefit is received before disclaiming.

A beneficiary cannot disclaim part of an interest — it is all or nothing.
Post-death variations.summary_line: DISCLAIMER · REFUSE INTEREST · NO REDIRECTION · AS IF PREDECEASED · s.93 IHTA · DEED OF VARIATION CONTRAST

Post-death variations.front: What conditions must be satisfied for a deed of variation to be effective for IHT purposes under s.142 IHTA 1984?
Post-death variations.answer:
A deed of variation (DOV) is treated for IHT purposes as if the deceased had made the varied disposition, **reading back** to the date of death, provided:

1. **Written instrument** — the variation must be in writing.
2. **Two-year window** — the instrument must be executed within **two years** of the deceased's death.
3. **Statement of intent** — the instrument must contain a statement by the parties that it is intended to have IHT effect under s.142 IHTA 1984. Without this election, the read-back does not apply for IHT.
4. **No prior variation** — the property being varied must not have already been the subject of a previous variation.
5. **Executed by all beneficiaries affected** — all those whose entitlement is reduced must be party to the instrument and must be adults with capacity.

If the variation increases the estate's IHT liability, HMRC must also be notified.

**Effect:** HMRC treats the estate as if it passed in the varied form from the date of death — useful for redirecting assets to charities (100% exemption) or to a surviving spouse (spouse exemption).
Post-death variations.summary_line: S.142 IHTA 1984 · DOV · TWO YEARS · WRITTEN · STATEMENT OF INTENT · READ-BACK TO DATE OF DEATH · NO PRIOR VARIATION

Post-death variations.front: How does s.62(6)–(7) TCGA 1992 apply to deeds of variation, and when is a separate CGT election required?
Post-death variations.answer:
Under s.62(6)–(7) TCGA 1992, assets passing on death are acquired by the beneficiary at **market value at the date of death** (no CGT charge at death). A deed of variation does not itself constitute a disposal by the original beneficiary for CGT purposes **if** a specific election is made.

**CGT election requirements:**
- The instrument must contain a **separate written election** that s.62(6) TCGA 1992 applies.
- This election is in **addition to** (not instead of) the IHT election under s.142 IHTA.
- The CGT election means the variation is treated as if the deceased had made the varied disposal — the new beneficiary acquires the asset at date-of-death probate value.

**What if no CGT election is made?**
- The variation is treated as a disposal by the original beneficiary to the new beneficiary at market value at the date of the DOV.
- If assets have risen in value between death and the DOV, the original beneficiary may incur a CGT liability.

**Practical point:** Always check whether both the IHT and CGT elections are needed. They apply independently — a DOV can elect for one but not the other.
Post-death variations.summary_line: S.62 TCGA 1992 · CGT ELECTION · SEPARATE FROM IHT ELECTION · NO DISPOSAL IF ELECTION MADE · PROBATE VALUE BASIS · RISING ASSETS RISK

Post-death variations.front: Can beneficiaries execute a deed of variation where one of the affected beneficiaries is a minor or lacks mental capacity?
Post-death variations.answer:
No — not without court approval. A deed of variation requires the **consent of all beneficiaries whose entitlements are varied**. A person under 18 lacks capacity to execute a DOV, and a person who lacks mental capacity cannot consent.

**Options where a minor or incapacitated beneficiary is involved:**

1. **Court approval under the Variation of Trusts Act 1958 (VTA 1958):** The court can approve a variation on behalf of minor or unborn beneficiaries if the variation is for their **benefit**. Benefit is broadly construed — may include tax savings for the family as a whole, even if the minor's individual entitlement is reduced, provided the overall benefit is real.

2. **Court approval under the Mental Capacity Act 2005 / Court of Protection:** For an adult lacking capacity, the Court of Protection may authorise the variation on behalf of that person if it is in their **best interests**.

**Note:** HMRC will still accept the DOV for IHT/CGT purposes if the court has authorised it on behalf of the incapacitated party — the read-back under s.142 IHTA 1984 / s.62 TCGA 1992 can still apply.

**Practical point:** Seek court approval early — it takes time and the two-year window for the DOV cannot be extended.
Post-death variations.summary_line: DOV MINOR BENEFICIARY · VTA 1958 COURT APPROVAL · BENEFIT TEST · MCA 2005 INCAPACITY · COURT OF PROTECTION · TWO-YEAR WINDOW STRICT

Post-death variations.front: Harry dies leaving his estate of £800,000 equally between his two adult children, Imogen and James. Imogen wants to redirect her share to her adult daughter, Lily, to reduce IHT. Harry died 14 months ago. Both children and Lily agree. What steps must be taken and what tax effect will the variation produce?
Post-death variations.answer:
**Steps:**
1. Execute a **deed of variation** within two years of Harry's death — 14 months have elapsed, leaving up to 10 months to execute. The two-year window is still open.
2. The instrument must be **in writing** and signed by all affected parties — Imogen (whose entitlement is reduced) and Lily (the new beneficiary). James is not affected so need not be a party.
3. Include the **s.142 IHTA 1984 election** to achieve IHT read-back.
4. Consider whether a **s.62 TCGA 1992 CGT election** is needed — if the assets in Imogen's share have risen since Harry's death, omitting it would create a CGT disposal by Imogen.

**IHT effect:**
With the read-back, HMRC treats Harry's estate as having passed £400,000 to Lily (not Imogen). This does not itself save IHT on Harry's estate (both Imogen and Lily are chargeable beneficiaries outside the exemptions), but it could be useful if Lily were a charity or if the purpose is to skip a generation and avoid IHT on Imogen's own estate later.

**No IHT saving here if Lily is not exempt** — but the DOV achieves estate planning by ensuring the asset passes directly from Harry's estate to Lily, without forming part of Imogen's estate for future IHT.
Post-death variations.summary_line: DOV · TWO YEARS NOT EXPIRED · S.142 IHTA ELECTION · S.62 TCGA ELECTION · GENERATION SKIP · NOT INHERENTLY IHT-FREE · ESTATE PLANNING TOOL

Post-death variations.front: Clare dies leaving her estate to her son David. Two years and three months after her death, David tries to execute a deed of variation to redirect part of the estate to his children. His solicitor advises this is out of time. David argues the solicitor can apply to court for an extension. Is the solicitor right and is the argument correct?
Post-death variations.answer:
The solicitor is correct and David's argument is wrong.

**The two-year window is absolute:** Section 142 IHTA 1984 requires the deed of variation to be executed within **two years of the deceased's death**. There is no discretion to extend this period and no mechanism for a court to grant an extension. The limitation is statutory and strictly applied.

**David cannot use a DOV** — it is now out of time. The statutory read-back under s.142 IHTA 1984 is unavailable. Executing a deed of variation now would not achieve IHT or CGT treatment as if the varied disposition had been made by Clare.

**What are the alternatives?**
- David could make **lifetime gifts** to his children from the inherited assets — these would be potentially exempt transfers (PETs) for IHT. If David survives seven years, no IHT is payable.
- A **trust** established by David could also be used, though this would be a chargeable lifetime transfer if a discretionary trust.
- There is **no retrospective relief** equivalent to the DOV available outside the two-year window.

**Key point:** Solicitors advising estates must flag the two-year window early and ensure clients know the deadline.
Post-death variations.summary_line: DOV OUT OF TIME · TWO YEARS ABSOLUTE · NO COURT EXTENSION · S.142 IHTA UNAVAILABLE · LIFETIME GIFT PET ALTERNATIVE · STRICT STATUTORY DEADLINE

Post-death variations.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A client says: 'I have signed a deed of variation this week redirecting my late mother's shares to my children. That means the shares have now transferred from me to them today and I'll be assessed to CGT on any gain since probate.' Correct any errors in the client's understanding.
Post-death variations.issue: The Trap: The client assumes a deed of variation operates as a present transfer from the original beneficiary to the new beneficiary, creating a disposal at today's market value and triggering CGT.
Post-death variations.rule: The Reality: A deed of variation with a valid s.62(6) TCGA 1992 CGT election does **not** constitute a disposal by the original beneficiary. Instead, the variation is treated as if the deceased had made the varied disposition — the new beneficiary acquires the asset at **probate value (date of death)**. There is no CGT disposal by the original beneficiary. Without the CGT election, however, the client's assumption is correct — it would be treated as a disposal at today's market value.
Post-death variations.application: Why the Distractor Fails: The client has overlooked the CGT election mechanism. The whole point of the election under s.62 TCGA 1992 is to avoid treating the variation as a disposal. The instruction to include 'a statement that s.62(6) TCGA 1992 applies' in the DOV is the step that prevents the CGT charge the client fears. The client's understanding would be correct only if the CGT election is omitted — which would be poor practice where the assets have appreciated since probate.
Post-death variations.conclusion: The Key Rule: DOV + s.62 TCGA election = no disposal by original beneficiary + new beneficiary acquires at probate value. DOV without CGT election = disposal at current market value + potential CGT for original beneficiary. Always include both elections (IHT and CGT) unless there is a specific reason not to.
Post-death variations.summary_line: DOV · S.62 TCGA ELECTION · NO DISPOSAL IF ELECTION MADE · PROBATE VALUE · WITHOUT ELECTION DISPOSAL AT MARKET VALUE · ALWAYS INCLUDE BOTH ELECTIONS

PR duties and the executor's year.subtopic: PR duties and the executor's year
PR duties and the executor's year.front: Yvette is the sole executor of her father's estate. He died 14 months ago. His daughter Zoe (a residuary beneficiary) is pressing Yvette to distribute the estate immediately. Yvette says she needs more time to resolve a disputed debt. Zoe threatens legal action. Advise Yvette.
PR duties and the executor's year.answer:
Yvette is in a defensible position, provided she has reasonable cause for the continued delay.

The executor's year: beneficiaries cannot compel distribution within one year of death. The year has now passed (14 months). After the executor's year, Zoe is entitled to seek an account and, if distribution is unreasonably delayed, to take action.

However: an unresolved disputed debt is a legitimate reason for delay. A PR who distributes before resolving creditor claims risks personal liability if the debt is later established and the estate is insufficient to meet it. Yvette is acting properly by waiting.

Advice to Yvette: (1) Communicate the reason for delay to Zoe in writing; (2) take active steps to resolve the disputed debt — obtain legal advice, consider making an interim distribution of the undisputed portion of the estate to Zoe while retaining a fund sufficient to meet the disputed claim; (3) consider applying for a Benjamin order if the creditor cannot be traced.
PR duties and the executor's year.summary_line: EXECUTOR'S YEAR · LEGITIMATE DELAY · DISPUTED DEBT · INTERIM DISTRIBUTION · COMMUNICATE

PR duties and the executor's year.subtopic: PR duties and the executor's year
PR duties and the executor's year.front: Alex is executor of his mother's estate. He sells his mother's investment portfolio during administration and, without taking advice, reinvests the proceeds in highly speculative shares which fall significantly in value. The residuary beneficiaries suffer a loss. Are they entitled to claim against Alex?
PR duties and the executor's year.answer:
Yes. As executor, Alex owes fiduciary duties to the beneficiaries — including a duty to invest prudently and to act in the beneficiaries' best interests.

An executor who makes speculative investments without proper authority and contrary to the standard of a prudent person of business may be liable for the loss caused: Learoyd v Whiteley (1887) / Trustee Act 2000.

Under the Trustee Act 2000, trustees (and PRs exercising investment powers) must apply a 'standard investment criteria' — suitability and diversification. They must also take proper advice unless it is unnecessary (s.5 TA 2000).

Alex failed to take advice and made speculative investments. The beneficiaries have a claim against him for the diminution in value caused by his breach of duty. Alex is personally liable to make good the loss — this is not a risk the estate bears.
PR duties and the executor's year.summary_line: PR INVESTMENT · SPECULATIVE · TRUSTEE ACT 2000 · STANDARD INVESTMENT CRITERIA · PERSONAL LIABILITY · LEAROYD V WHITELEY

Order of debts (solvent and insolvent).front: Ben's estate is worth £120,000. His will leaves £60,000 to his friend Cara (general legacy) and the residue to charity. He has debts of £90,000. How are the debts and legacies satisfied?
Order of debts (solvent and insolvent).answer:
Step 1 — Pay debts first (£90,000). Remaining: £120,000 − £90,000 = £30,000.

Step 2 — Apply the statutory order (Sch.1 AEA 1925). Where the estate is solvent but insufficient to meet all legacies, assets are applied in this order:
1. Residue — charity's residuary gift is used first. The entire £30,000 remaining is in residue; charity takes nothing.
2. General legacies — Cara's £60,000 general legacy abates. Only £30,000 is available. Cara receives £30,000.

Summary: Cara receives £30,000 (abated from £60,000); charity receives nothing. The estate is solvent — all creditors are paid in full — but the legacies abate to meet the shortfall.
Order of debts (solvent and insolvent).summary_line: ABATEMENT · SOLVENT ESTATE · RESIDUE BEARS SHORTFALL · GENERAL LEGACY ABATES · CALCULATION

Order of debts (solvent and insolvent).front: Dan dies with an estate worth £50,000. His liabilities are: funeral expenses £5,000; an unsecured bank loan £30,000; unpaid wages to a former employee £8,000 (a preferential debt). His estate is insolvent. In what order are the debts paid?
Order of debts (solvent and insolvent).answer:
The estate is insolvent (liabilities exceed assets). The Insolvency Act 1986 order applies:

1. Secured creditors — none on the facts.
2. Funeral expenses: £5,000. (Reasonable funeral expenses are given statutory priority.) Remaining: £45,000.
3. Testamentary and administration expenses: assumed nil on the facts. Remaining: £45,000.
4. Preferential debts — unpaid employee wages (£8,000): paid in full. Remaining: £37,000.
5. Ordinary unsecured debts — bank loan £30,000: paid in full from the remaining £37,000. Remaining: £7,000.

On these facts the estate is actually sufficient to pay all creditors. If it were truly insolvent (liabilities greater than £50,000), ordinary unsecured creditors would be paid pari passu — proportionally — with any shortfall falling on them.

Beneficiaries receive nothing from an insolvent estate.
Order of debts (solvent and insolvent).summary_line: INSOLVENT ESTATE · INSOLVENCY ACT ORDER · FUNERAL · PREFERENTIAL · UNSECURED · BENEFICIARIES NOTHING

PETs and CLTs.front: What is a potentially exempt transfer (PET) and when does it become chargeable?
PETs and CLTs.answer:
A potentially exempt transfer (PET) is a lifetime transfer of value from one individual to another individual (or certain trusts for disabled persons): s.3A IHTA 1984.

A PET is exempt from IHT if the donor survives for seven years after making it. If the donor dies within seven years, the PET becomes chargeable — it is brought back into the estate and charged to IHT.

Taper relief reduces the IHT on the PET where the donor survived between three and seven years (reducing the tax by 20%–80% on a sliding scale).

PETs are treated as exempt when made — no IHT is paid at the time. IHT only crystallises on death within the seven-year window. The PET uses up the nil rate band of the estate in chronological order.
PETs and CLTs.summary_line: PET · s.3A IHTA 1984 · INDIVIDUAL TO INDIVIDUAL · NO LIFETIME TAX · SEVEN YEAR RULE · TAPER RELIEF

PETs and CLTs.front: What is a chargeable lifetime transfer (CLT) and how is it taxed?
PETs and CLTs.answer:
A chargeable lifetime transfer (CLT) is a transfer of value that is not a PET and not covered by an exemption. The most common CLT is a transfer into a discretionary trust.

Taxation:
- IHT is payable at 20% (half the death rate of 40%) on the amount exceeding the available nil rate band at the time of the CLT.
- If the donor dies within seven years, further IHT is charged at the full 40% death rate, with a credit for the 20% paid during the donor's lifetime. The additional charge is subject to taper relief if death occurs between 3–7 years.
- CLTs are cumulated with earlier CLTs and PETs made within the preceding seven years when calculating the available nil rate band.

The donee (trustee) pays the tax unless the donor agrees to pay it (in which case grossing up applies).
PETs and CLTs.summary_line: CLT · DISCRETIONARY TRUST · 20% LIFETIME · 40% ON DEATH · CREDIT FOR LIFETIME TAX · TAPER · CUMULATION

PETs and CLTs.front: How are PETs and CLTs brought into account on death, and in what order are they set against the nil rate band?
PETs and CLTs.answer:
On death, all chargeable transfers made in the seven years before death are cumulated and set against the nil rate band (NRB) in chronological order — oldest first.

Order of application:
1. CLTs within the seven-year period (using NRB first, with any excess charged at 20% during lifetime);
2. PETs within the seven-year period (now chargeable on death — set against remaining NRB);
3. The death estate (charged at 40% on the excess after applying remaining NRB).

If earlier transfers have exhausted the NRB, the death estate is charged in full at 40% with no NRB available.

Key trap: the order matters. A large CLT shortly before death may exhaust the NRB entirely, exposing later PETs and the estate to full IHT at 40%.
PETs and CLTs.summary_line: NRB CUMULATION · CHRONOLOGICAL ORDER · PETS AND CLTS · SEVEN YEARS · OLDEST FIRST · DEATH ESTATE LAST

PETs and CLTs.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

In March 2022, Rachel gives her daughter £200,000. Rachel's accountant tells Rachel she must pay IHT on the gift immediately because it exceeds the nil rate band (after accounting for prior transfers). Rachel pays 40% IHT on the excess. Rachel survives the gift by more than seven years. Was the accountant right to charge IHT at the time of the gift?
PETs and CLTs.issue: The Trap: the student (or accountant) treats a gift from one individual to another as immediately chargeable to IHT at the time of transfer. The scenario is designed to induce confusion between PETs and CLTs.
PETs and CLTs.rule: The Reality: a gift from one individual to another individual is a potentially exempt transfer (PET) under s.3A IHTA 1984. PETs are treated as exempt when made — no IHT is payable at the time of the gift. IHT only becomes payable on a PET if the donor dies within seven years.
PETs and CLTs.application: Why the Distractor Fails: the 20% lifetime charge and the immediate 40% charge apply only to chargeable lifetime transfers (CLTs) — most commonly transfers into discretionary trusts. A direct gift from Rachel to her daughter is a PET, not a CLT. No IHT was due in March 2022. Since Rachel survives seven years, the PET becomes fully exempt and no IHT is payable at all. The accountant was wrong — and Rachel should seek a refund of any IHT incorrectly paid.
PETs and CLTs.conclusion: The Key Rule: gifts between individuals are PETs — no IHT is payable at the time of transfer. IHT only crystallises on a PET if the donor dies within seven years. Only CLTs (typically transfers into discretionary trusts) attract a lifetime charge.
PETs and CLTs.summary_line: PET · NO LIFETIME CHARGE · INDIVIDUAL TO INDIVIDUAL · CLT DISTINCTION · SEVEN YEAR RULE

Nil rate band and RNRB.front: What is the nil rate band (NRB) and how does it operate?
Nil rate band and RNRB.answer:
The nil rate band (NRB) is the threshold below which no IHT is charged: s.7 IHTA 1984. It is currently £325,000 (frozen until at least April 2028).

The NRB applies to the cumulative value of all chargeable transfers in the seven years before death (including CLTs and failed PETs), plus the death estate.

IHT is charged at 40% on the value of the estate exceeding the available NRB.

Transferability: where a spouse or civil partner does not use their full NRB on death (e.g. all estate passes to the surviving spouse exempt), the unused proportion is transferable to the survivor's estate on their later death: s.8A IHTA 1984. This can give a combined NRB of up to £650,000.
Nil rate band and RNRB.summary_line: NRB · £325K · s.7 IHTA 1984 · TRANSFERABLE BETWEEN SPOUSES · UNUSED PROPORTION · s.8A · £650K COMBINED

Nil rate band and RNRB.front: What is the residence nil rate band (RNRB) and when does it apply?
Nil rate band and RNRB.answer:
The residence nil rate band (RNRB) is an additional NRB of up to £175,000 (currently) available where:

1. The deceased's estate includes a qualifying residential interest (a property the deceased lived in as their home at some point); and
2. The property is 'closely inherited' — passed to a direct descendant (children, grandchildren, stepchildren, adopted children) or their spouses/civil partners: s.8H IHTA 1984.

Taper: the RNRB is reduced by £1 for every £2 by which the net estate exceeds £2 million, disappearing entirely at £2.35 million.

Transferability: unused RNRB is transferable between spouses/civil partners on the same basis as the NRB.

Maximum combined NRB + RNRB per individual: £500,000 (£1 million for a surviving spouse with both transferred bands).
Nil rate band and RNRB.summary_line: RNRB · £175K · DIRECT DESCENDANTS · QUALIFYING RESIDENTIAL INTEREST · £2M TAPER · TRANSFERABLE · s.8H

Nil rate band and RNRB.front: H dies in 2020 leaving his entire estate to his wife W. W dies in 2025 with an estate of £900,000 including the family home (worth £400,000), left to their two children. How much IHT is payable?
Nil rate band and RNRB.answer:
Step 1 — Available NRB for W:
- W's own NRB: £325,000
- Transferred NRB from H (100% unused — estate passed entirely to W): £325,000
- Combined NRB: £650,000

Step 2 — Available RNRB for W:
- W's own RNRB: £175,000
- Transferred RNRB from H (100% unused): £175,000
- Combined RNRB: £350,000
- The home (£400,000) passes to direct descendants ✓ — RNRB applies.

Step 3 — Chargeable estate:
£900,000 − £650,000 (NRB) − £350,000 (RNRB) = −£100,000 → nil charge.

IHT payable: £0.

Note: the RNRB cannot exceed the value of the residential property (£400,000 > £350,000 ✓, so full RNRB is available). The estate is below the £2m taper threshold.
Nil rate band and RNRB.summary_line: RNRB CALCULATION · TRANSFERRED NRB · TRANSFERRED RNRB · £1M COMBINED · TAPER · DIRECT DESCENDANTS

Nil rate band and RNRB.front: Going deeper: The residence nil rate band (RNRB), introduced in 2017, has been criticised as a regressive and poorly designed relief that entrenches intergenerational wealth inequality. Evaluate this critique.
Nil rate band and RNRB.answer:
The design of the RNRB

The RNRB (ss.8H–8M IHTA 1984) provides up to £175,000 additional relief (transferable between spouses) where a qualifying residential interest passes to direct descendants. Combined with the NRB, a married couple can pass up to £1 million free of IHT.

The policy rationale

The RNRB was introduced by the Conservative government partly in response to political pressure — house price inflation had brought many middle-income estates within the IHT threshold despite the 'death tax' being politically associated with the very wealthy. The government's stated aim was to allow parents to pass the family home to their children free of IHT.

The regressivity critique

Professor Judith Freedman (Oxford, tax law) and the Institute for Fiscal Studies have argued the RNRB is poorly targeted:

1. It benefits only those with property: renters, however wealthy in other assets, cannot access the RNRB. This discriminates between asset classes without principled justification.
2. It incentivises retaining property over other assets: a testator may hold an illiquid property rather than sell and invest the proceeds, distorting economic decisions.
3. The taper threshold (£2 million) means the relief is withdrawn from the very wealthy — but the withdrawal is linear rather than graduated, producing a marginal effective rate above 40% for estates just over £2 million.
4. The restriction to direct descendants excludes childless testators, same-sex couples with no children, and those who leave property to other relatives.

The broader IHT critique

The Office of Tax Simplification (now abolished) recommended in 2018 that the RNRB be abolished and the NRB increased instead — a simpler and more neutral approach. The government declined. The RNRB remains in place, frozen until at least 2028, making it increasingly valuable in real terms as property prices rise.

Exemptions.front: What are the main IHT exemptions applicable to both lifetime transfers and on death?
Exemptions.answer:
1. Spouse/civil partner exemption: transfers between spouses/civil partners are wholly exempt — no limit, provided both are UK-domiciled: s.18 IHTA 1984. Where the recipient is non-UK domiciled, the exemption is capped (currently £325,000).

2. Charity exemption: gifts to qualifying UK charities are wholly exempt: s.23 IHTA 1984. A reduced rate of 36% applies where at least 10% of the net estate is left to charity.

3. Annual exemption: the first £3,000 of transfers per tax year is exempt: s.19 IHTA 1984. Any unused annual exemption can be carried forward one year only.

4. Small gifts exemption: gifts to any one person of £250 or less per tax year are exempt: s.20 IHTA 1984. Cannot be combined with the annual exemption for the same recipient.

5. Normal expenditure out of income: regular gifts from surplus income are exempt if they form part of a normal pattern and do not reduce the donor's standard of living: s.21 IHTA 1984.
Exemptions.summary_line: IHT EXEMPTIONS · SPOUSE · CHARITY · ANNUAL £3K · SMALL GIFTS £250 · NORMAL EXPENDITURE OUT OF INCOME
